useSystemReducedMotion() {\n  const [reduced, setReduced] = useState(false);\n  useEffect(() => {\n    const query = window.matchMedia(\"(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)\");\n    const update = () => setReduced(query.matches);\n    update();\n    query.addEventListener(\"change\", update);\n    return () => query.removeEventListener(\"change\", update);\n  }, []);\n  return reduced;\n}\n\r\nimport type { AnimationOwner } from \"./animation-types\";\n\ntype PropertyOwner = { owner: AnimationOwner; properties: Set<string> };\nconst registry = new WeakMap<Element, PropertyOwner[]>();\n\nexport function claimAnimationOwnership(element: Element, owner: AnimationOwner, properties: readonly string[]) {\n  const claims = registry.get(element) ?? [];\n  const conflict = claims.find(\n    (claim) => claim.owner !== owner && properties.some((property) => claim.properties.has(property)),\n  );\n  if (conflict) {\n    if (process.env.NODE_ENV !== \"production\") {\n      console.warn(\n        `[animation ownership] ${owner} cannot claim ${properties.join(\", \")}; ${conflict.owner} already owns a conflicting property.`,\n        element,\n      );\n    }\n    return false;\n  }\n  const existing = claims.find((claim) => claim.owner === owner);\n  if (existing) properties.forEach((property) => existing.properties.add(property));\n  else claims.push({ owner, properties: new Set(properties) });\n  registry.set(element, claims);\n  element.setAttribute(\"data-animation-owner\", owner);\n  return true;\n}\n\nexport function releaseAnimationOwnership(element: Element, owner: AnimationOwner) {\n  const remaining = (registry.get(element) ?? []).filter((claim) => claim.owner !== owner);\n  if (remaining.length) {\n    registry.set(element, remaining);\n    element.setAttribute(\"data-animation-owner\", remaining.at(-1)!.owner);\n  } else {\n    registry.delete(element);\n    element.removeAttribute(\"data-animation-owner\");\n  }\n}\n\nexport function animationOwnerFor(element: Element, property: string) {\n  return registry.get(element)?.find((claim) => claim.properties.has(property))?.owner ?? null;\n}\n\nexport function claimSceneTargets(\n  root: HTMLElement,\n  selector: string,\n  owner: AnimationOwner,\n  properties: readonly string[],\n) {\n  const targets = Array.from(root.querySelectorAll(selector));\n  const claimed = targets.filter((target) => claimAnimationOwnership(target, owner, properties));\n  return () => claimed.forEach((target) => releaseAnimationOwnership(target, owner));\n}\n\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

For each proposed addition include:\\r\\n\\r\\n```text\\r\\nScreen\\r\\nMoment\\r\\nUser benefit\\r\\nVisual concept\\r\\nCorrect library\\r\\nSupporting library if any\\r\\nTrigger\\r\\nDuration range\\r\\nReplay policy\\r\\nReduced-motion behavior\\r\\nSound cue\\r\\nFailure fallback\\r\\nPriority\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nInclude additions for at least:\\r\\n\\r\\n* Landing and role selection\\r\\n* Player sign-in\\r\\n* Captain sign-in\\r\\n* Creator sign-in\\r\\n* Invitation ceremony\\r\\n* Player Library\\r\\n* Captain Library\\r\\n* New Voyage wizard\\r\\n* Waiting room\\r\\n* Journal opening\\r\\n* Journal pages\\r\\n* Chapter release\\r\\n* Chart\\r\\n* Treasure Altar\\r\\n* Artifact inspection\\r\\n* Side quests\\r\\n* Shipâ€™s Log\\r\\n* Finale\\r\\n* Quartermaster commands\\r\\n* Studio authoring\\r\\n* loading/error/success states\\r\\n* shell navigation\\r\\n* route transitions\\r\\n* theme/color-scheme transitions\\r\\n* offline/reconnect\\r\\n* notification and unseen-content states\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not reduce the list to ten or twenty items. The goal is a comprehensive experience blueprint.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 10. Required Deliverables\\r\\n\\r\\nCreate the following files inside `Development_Docs`:\\r\\n\\r\\n## 10.1 Master audit\\r\\n\\r\\n```text\\r\\nDevelopment_Docs/Animation_System_Full_Audit.md\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nThis must contain:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Executive summary\\r\\n2. Audit baseline and methodology\\r\\n3. Current architecture\\r\\n4. Current library inventory\\r\\n5. Scene reachability report\\r\\n6. Animations that do not run\\r\\n7. Animations that run invisibly\\r\\n8. Animations that run poorly\\r\\n9. One-shot and replay analysis\\r\\n10. Ownership conflicts\\r\\n11. Wrong-library findings\\r\\n12. Lifecycle and cleanup findings\\r\\n13. Reduced-motion findings\\r\\n14. Performance findings\\r\\n15. Per-screen audit\\r\\n16. Per-library audit\\r\\n17. Massive missing-animation list\\r\\n18. Recommended architecture\\r\\n19. Severity-ranked findings\\r\\n20. Evidence references\\r\\n21. Limitations and items requiring further runtime confirmation\\r\\n\\r\\n## 10.2 Detailed animation matrix\\r\\n\\r\\n```text\\r\\nDevelopment_Docs/Animation_System_Audit_Matrix.csv\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nThe CSV must be genuinely useful for implementation planning and must contain the required fields specified earlier.\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not create a CSV containing vague one-line summaries.\\r\\n\\r\\n## 10.3 Implementation roadmap\\r\\n\\r\\n```text\\r\\nDevelopment_Docs/Animation_System_Implementation_Roadmap.md\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nOrganize implementation into no more than six major phases.\\r\\n\\r\\nA recommended phase structure is:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. **Animation truth and broken-trigger repair**\\r\\n2. **Ownership and scene-scoping architecture**\\r\\n3. **Player cinematic integration**\\r\\n4. **Platform, Library and Invitation motion**\\r\\n5. **Rive/Lottie/PageFlip completion**\\r\\n6. **Performance, accessibility and final polish**\\r\\n\\r\\nFor each phase include:\\r\\n\\r\\n* exact scope,\\r\\n* dependencies,\\r\\n* files likely affected,\\r\\n* libraries involved,\\r\\n* acceptance criteria,\\r\\n* tests,\\r\\n* risks,\\r\\n* work that can happen in parallel,\\r\\n* work that must not happen in parallel.\\r\\n\\r\\n## 10.4 Animation test plan\\r\\n\\r\\n```text\\r\\nDevelopment_Docs/Animation_System_Test_Plan.md\\r\\n```\\r\\n\\r\\nInclude:\\r\\n\\r\\n* unit tests,\\r\\n* component tests,\\r\\n* end-to-end tests,\\r\\n* visual checkpoint tests,\\r\\n* target-integrity tests,\\r\\n* replay tests,\\r\\n* one-shot tests,\\r\\n* every-event/every-section matrix,\\r\\n* mode matrix,\\r\\n* viewport matrix,\\r\\n* interruption tests,\\r\\n* performance tests,\\r\\n* memory-leak tests,\\r\\n* fallback tests.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 11. Severity and Priority Rules\\r\\n\\r\\nUse these severity levels:\\r\\n\\r\\n## P0 - Broken core experience\\r\\n\\r\\nExamples:\\r\\n\\r\\n* a major ceremony is invisible,\\r\\n* animation blocks interaction indefinitely,\\r\\n* route cannot complete,\\r\\n* reduced-motion accessibility failure,\\r\\n* production asset silently does not exist,\\r\\n* server event is acknowledged despite no visible required presentation when presentation is mandatory.\\r\\n\\r\\n## P1 - Major quality or consistency failure\\r\\n\\r\\nExamples:\\r\\n\\r\\n* wrong object animates,\\r\\n* duplicate objects animate,\\r\\n* replay is unavailable,\\r\\n* animation differs unpredictably by section,\\r\\n* severe library ownership collision,\\r\\n* visible snapback,\\r\\n* page-turn setting does not apply.\\r\\n\\r\\n## P2 - Noticeable polish deficiency\\r\\n\\r\\nExamples:\\r\\n\\r\\n* weak easing,\\r\\n* generic fade,\\r\\n* missing secondary motion,\\r\\n* missing form transition,\\r\\n* poor loading state,\\r\\n* abrupt filtering.\\r\\n\\r\\n## P3 - Enhancement\\r\\n\\r\\nExamples:\\r\\n\\r\\n* richer ambient motion,\\r\\n* optional parallax,\\r\\n* subtle hover refinement,\\r\\n* additional material response.\\r\\n\\r\\nPrioritize correctness and consistency before adding decorative layers.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 12. Required Final Recommendations\\r\\n\\r\\nThe final audit must answer all of the following:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Which animations are completely broken?\\r\\n2. Which animations do not run in production?\\r\\n3. Which animations run only in the development showcase?\\r\\n4. Which animations run but target invisible elements?\\r\\n5. Which animations depend on the Playerâ€™s current section?\\r\\n6. Which animations run once?\\r\\n7. Which one-shot policies are correct?\\r\\n8. Which one-shot policies should change?\\r\\n9. Which animations cannot replay after refresh?\\r\\n10. Which scenes have no real trigger?\\r\\n11. Which scenes should be retired?\\r\\n12. Which scenes should be connected?\\r\\n13. Which behaviors use the wrong library?\\r\\n14. Which nodes have conflicting owners?\\r\\n15. Which Rive objects are currently static fallbacks?\\r\\n16. Which Lottie effects restart incorrectly?\\r\\n17. Which PageFlip behaviors fail after mode changes?\\r\\n18. Which CSS phase waits can hang or skip?\\r\\n19. Which animations are missing from modern platform screens?\\r\\n20. What should be added to make the entire experience feel unified and premium?\\r\\n21. What exact implementation sequence should follow?\\r\\n22. Which work can be done in parallel?\\r\\n23. What automated tests are required before declaring the animation system complete?\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 13. Hard Boundaries\\r\\n\\r\\nDo not:\\r\\n\\r\\n* broadly implement fixes during this audit,\\r\\n* rewrite the entire animation architecture,\\r\\n* replace all libraries with GSAP,\\r\\n* replace all libraries with Motion,\\r\\n* delete Rive because final assets are missing,\\r\\n* replace StPageFlip with a homegrown book animation,\\r\\n* change unrelated product functionality,\\r\\n* alter narrative or personal content,\\r\\n* expose private content,\\r\\n* interfere with concurrent animation-fix work,\\r\\n* stage the entire repository,\\r\\n* claim runtime confirmation without actually running the relevant flow,\\r\\n* claim an animation works merely because a timeline completed,\\r\\n* claim a fallback is a real Rive animation,\\r\\n* produce only a short summary.\\r\\n\\r\\nTemporary non-invasive instrumentation is acceptable if required for investigation, but it must either:\\r\\n\\r\\n* remain isolated and clearly documented, or\\r\\n* be reverted before task completion.\\r\\n\\r\\nThe final committed application source should remain unchanged unless there is an extremely small audit-support correction that is separately justified. Documentation files are the intended output.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 14. Quality Bar\\r\\n\\r\\nThe audit is complete only when:\\r\\n\\r\\n* every registered scene has a reachability status,\\r\\n* every real animation call site has been inventoried,\\r\\n* every production animation library has a status,\\r\\n* all known hypotheses have been verified or disproved,\\r\\n* one-shot and replay behavior is documented per animation,\\r\\n* broken and invisible animation paths are identified,\\r\\n* ownership conflicts are mapped,\\r\\n* the modern platform and legacy cinematic paths are compared,\\r\\n* every major screen has a missing-animation blueprint,\\r\\n* the audit matrix is extensive,\\r\\n* the implementation roadmap is actionable,\\r\\n* and the test plan would prevent the same regressions from returning.\\r\\n\\r\\nThe result should be detailed enough that future Codex implementation prompts can use the audit as the governing source of truth and implement one phase at a time without guessing.\\r\\n\\r\\n---\\r\\n\\r\\n# 15. Final Response Format\\r\\n\\r\\nAt completion, provide:\\r\\n\\r\\n1. Exact baseline audited.\\r\\n2. Git/worktree safety status.\\r\\n3. Count of animation items inventoried.\\r\\n4. Count by category:\\r\\n\\r\\n   * broken,\\r\\n   * invisible,\\r\\n   * poor quality,\\r\\n   * one-shot,\\r\\n   * replay-defective,\\r\\n   * wrong library,\\r\\n   * ownership conflict,\\r\\n   * missing animation,\\r\\n   * needs runtime verification.\\r\\n5. Top P0 findings.\\r\\n6. Top P1 findings.\\r\\n7. Deliverable file paths.\\r\\n8. Tests and runtime flows executed.\\r\\n9. Anything that could not be verified.\\r\\n10. \"../core/animation-types\";\nimport { scaledDistance, scaledDuration } from \"../core/quality\";\nimport { gsap } from \"../core/gsap-client\";\n\nexport function sceneTimeline(context: SceneBuildContext) {\n  const timeline = gsap.timeline({\n    paused: true,\n    defaults: { ease: \"forever-settle\", overwrite: \"auto\" },\n  });\n  mark(timeline, context, \"scene-start\", 0);\n  return timeline;\n}\n\nexport function mark(timeline: SceneTimeline, context: SceneBuildContext, label: string, position?: gsap.Position) {\n  timeline.addLabel(label, position);\n  timeline.call(() => context.emitLabel(label), [], label);\n  return label;\n}\n\nexport function parts(root: HTMLElement, ...names: string[]) {\n  return names.flatMap((name) => Array.from(root.querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>(`[data-scene-part=\"${name}\"]`)));\n}\n\nexport function seconds(context: SceneBuildContext, value: number) {\n  return scaledDuration(value, context.mode);\n}\n\nexport function distance(context: SceneBuildContext, value: number) {\n  return scaledDistance(value, context.mode);\n}\n\nexport function settle(timeline: SceneTimeline, context: SceneBuildContext, position: gsap.Position = \">\") {\n  const label = mark(timeline, context, \"interaction-restored\", position);\n  timeline.to(context.root, { duration: seconds(context, 0.08) }, label);\n  mark(timeline, context, \"scene-complete\", \">\");\n  return timeline;\n}\n\nexport function idleTimeline(context: SceneBuildContext, targetNames: string[] = [\"living-idle\"]) {\n  const timeline = sceneTimeline(context);\n  const targets = parts(context.root, ...targetNames);\n  mark(timeline, context, \"await-server\", 0);\n  if (targets.length) {\n    timeline.to(targets, {\n      opacity: 0.72,\n      scale: context.mode === \"reduced\" ? 1 : 1.025,\n      duration: seconds(context, 0.8),\n      repeat: -1,\n      yoyo: true,\n      ease: \"sine.inOut\",\n    });\n  } else timeline.to(context.root, { duration: seconds(context, 0.2), repeat: -1 });\n  return timeline;\n}\n\r\nexport const audioCueNames = [\n  \"ocean-rise\",\n  \"wood-creak\",\n  \"lantern-ignition\",\n  \"brass-latch\",\n  \"seal-pressure\",\n  \"wax-crack\",\n  \"page-turn\",\n  \"paper-flutter\",\n  \"quill-scratch\",\n  \"stamp-impact\",\n  \"compass-click\",\n  \"map-scratch\",\n  \"artifact-chime\",\n  \"mechanism-hum\",\n  \"pause-wind-down\",\n  \"undo-reverse\",\n] as const;\n\nexport type AudioCueName = (typeof audioCueNames)[number];\n\nconst frequencies: Record<AudioCueName, [number, number, number]> = {\n  \"ocean-rise\": [48, 72, 1.1],\n  \"wood-creak\": [96, 64, 0.45],\n  \"lantern-ignition\": [180, 320, 0.22],\n  \"brass-latch\": [240, 92, 0.18],\n  \"seal-pressure\": [82, 54, 0.42],\n  \"wax-crack\": [136, 44, 0.24],\n  \"page-turn\": [220, 110, 0.32],\n  \"paper-flutter\": [310, 170, 0.28],\n  \"quill-scratch\": [420, 360, 0.18],\n  \"stamp-impact\": [118, 46, 0.3],\n  \"compass-click\": [520, 260, 0.12],\n  \"map-scratch\": [360, 210, 0.32],\n  \"artifact-chime\": [440, 880, 0.7],\n  \"mechanism-hum\": [58, 92, 0.9],\n  \"pause-wind-down\": [120, 42, 0.68],\n  \"undo-reverse\": [210, 74, 0.5],\n};\n\nexport class AudioCuePlayer {\n  private context: AudioContext | null = null;\n  private active = new Set<{ oscillator: OscillatorNode; gain: GainNode }>();\n  private volume = 0.4;\n  private muted = false;\n\n  unlock() {\n    try {\n      this.context ??= new AudioContext();\n      if (this.context.state === \"suspended\") {\n        void this.context.resume().catch(() => {\n          // Audio is optional; a blocked device must never hold the journal closed.\n        });\n      }\n    } catch {\n      this.context = null;\n    }\n  }\n\n  setVolume(volume: number) {\n    this.volume = Math.max(0, Math.min(1, volume));\n  }\n\n  setMuted(muted: boolean) {\n    this.muted = muted;\n  }\n\n  play(name: AudioCueName) {\n    if (!this.context || this.muted || this.volume <= 0) return;\n    const [start, end, duration] = frequencies[name];\n    const oscillator = this.context.createOscillator();\n    const gain = this.context.createGain();\n    const now = this.context.currentTime;\n    oscillator.type = name.includes(\"chime\") || name.includes(\"compass\") ? \"sine\" : \"triangle\";\n    oscillator.frequency.setValueAtTime(start, now);\n    oscillator.frequency.exponentialRampToValueAtTime(Math.max(1, end), now + duration);\n    gain.gain.setValueAtTime(Math.max(0.001, this.volume * 0.11), now);\n    gain.gain.exponentialRampToValueAtTime(0.001, now + duration);\n    oscillator.connect(gain).connect(this.context.destination);\n    const entry = { oscillator, gain };\n    this.active.add(entry);\n    oscillator.onended = () => this.active.delete(entry);\n    oscillator.start(now);\n    oscillator.stop(now + duration + 0.02);\n  }\n\n  stopAll() {\n    if (!this.context) return;\n    const now = this.context.currentTime;\n    this.active.forEach(({ oscillator, gain }) => {\n      gain.gain.cancelScheduledValues(now);\n      gain.gain.setTargetAtTime(0.001, now, 0.025);\n      try {\n        oscillator.stop(now + 0.12);\n      } catch {\n        // The cue may already have reached its natural end.\n      }\n    });\n  }\n\n  close() {\n    this.stopAll();\n    void this.context?.close();\n    this.context = null;\n  }\n}\n\r\n=== TEST Intended: {scene['targets']}\",\r\n            \"Missing targets\": \"Dedicated scene host; required visible target declaration; instance ID\",\r\n            \"Duplicate targets\": \"Generic names may match permanent section, temporary overlay, hidden source, and clone\" if \"ownership_conflict\" in scene[\"cats\"] else \"Not applicable in production because scene is uncalled\",\r\n            \"Ownership conflicts\": \"GSAP claims are warning-only and Motion does not register in the runtime registry\" if \"ownership_conflict\" in scene[\"cats\"] else \"None observed because production trigger is absent\",\r\n            \"Cleanup status\": \"GSAP context reverts; final-state and route handoff are not explicitly declared\",\r\n            \"Fallback status\": \"Timeline can silently become a no-target timing sequence\",\r\n            \"Performance concerns\": \"Broad root query and duplicate-node animation\" if \"ownership_conflict\" in scene[\"cats\"] else \"Dead registry/showcase maintenance cost\",\r\n            \"Accessibility concerns\": \"Narrative completion can be acknowledged without visible/readable presentation\" if not showcase_only else \"No production impact until connected\",\r\n            \"Current quality\": scene[\"quality\"],\r\n            \"Failure severity\": scene[\"severity\"],\r\n            \"Recommended change\": \"Add a dedicated visible scene host, instance-scoped selectors, required-target assertions, persisted replay payload, and final-state policy. \" + scene[\"note\"],\r\n            \"Implementation complexity\": \"High\" if scene[\"severity\"] == \"P0\" else \"Medium\",\r\n            \"Implementation priority\": scene[\"severity\"],\r\n            \"Test coverage\": \"All-mode builder test; reduced showcase smoke. 8.5 Lottie audit\r\n1110: \r\n1111: Document:\r\n1112: \r\n1113: * every JSON asset,\r\n1114: * every mount,\r\n1115: * autoplay,\r\n1116: * loop,\r\n1117: * speed,\r\n1118: * visibility pause,\r\n1119: * document pause,\r\n1120: * mode changes,\r\n1121: * failure handling,\r\n1122: * stalled-load handling,\r\n1123: * timing relative to GSAP scenes.\r\n1124: \r\n1125: Identify non-looping Lottie effects that begin before their intended semantic scene label.\r\n1126: \r\n1127: ## 8.6 CSS audit\r\n1128: \r\n1129: Inventory:\r\n1130: \r\n1131: * all keyframes,\r\n1132: * all long transitions,\r\n1133: * data-attribute state machines,\r\n1134: * journal opening mechanics,\r\n1135: * waiting-room breathing,\r\n1136: * ambient effects,\r\n1137: * focus/hover transitions,\r\n1138: * reduced-motion overrides.\r\n1139: \r\n1140: Separate:\r\n1141: \r\n1142: * material rendering,\r\n1143: * ambient animation,\r\n1144: * narrative orchestration.\r\n1145: \r\n1146: Recommend moving narrative ordering out of CSS without removing CSS material behavior.\r\n1147: \r\n1148: ## 8.7 Web Animations API audit\r\n1149: \r\n1150: Audit:\r\n1151: \r\n1152: * use of `getAnimations`,\r\n1153: * handling of `animation.finished`,\r\n1154: * abort,\r\n1155: * timeout,\r\n1156: * infinite animations,\r\n1157: * missing animation detection,\r\n1158: * expected actor validation.\r\n1159: \r\n1160: ## 8.8 Audio audit\r\n1161: \r\n1162: Map every sound cue to:\r\n1163: \r\n1164: * current trigger,\r\n1165: * desired semantic label,\r\n1166: * timing offset,\r\n1167: * missing visual synchronization,\r\n1168: * reduced/silent behavior.\r\n1169: \r\n1170: ---\r\n1171: \r\n1172: # 9. Required Missing-Animation Blueprint\r\n1173: \r\n1174: The final report must include a massive list of animations that should be added.\r\n1175: \r\n1456: # 15. empty-state enhancements after correctness work.\"],\n+    ],\n+)\n+\n+master = f\"\"\"# Animation System Full Audit\n+\n+Status: complete audit of exact committed baseline `{BASELINE}`. Documentation generated from the controlling matrix; no application source fix is included.\n+\n+## 1. Executive summary\n+\n+The baseline has a substantial animation framework, but it does not yet have animation truth. The audit inventories **{len(rows)}** current effects, runtime contracts, and missing opportunities. Of the 28 registered GSAP scenes, **{len(production_scenes)}** have a production call path and **{len(showcase_only)}** are development-showcase-only, dead, or redundant. All 28 can build and finish without a required visible target. Therefore a completed timeline, a green builder test, and a clean showcase run do not prove that a user saw the intended object.\n+\n+The most serious chain is in Player progression: root-scoped scenes query whichever section happens to be mounted; generic part names can match permanent content, a temporary event overlay, PageFlip sources, or PageFlip clones; presentation errors are caught; the snapshot refresh and viewed acknowledgment still continue. A mandatory event can consequently be marked viewed even when no required content was visible. Chapter release also keeps replay only in `lastRelease` component state, so refresh loses it. The live baseline run did render seal, parchment, and ink stages and exposed Replay Ceremony, but measured **10,081 ms**, failing the existing `<10,000 ms` acceptance limit.\n+\n+All four named production Rive contracts are fallbacks: their contracts omit a `.riv` path, the only `.riv` is a development sample, and two contracts are not mounted. Modern sign-in, Invitation Ceremony, Library, waiting-room, and Studio flows largely bypass the legacy cinematic paths and lack a unified Motion policy. Reduced motion has three competing authorities: custom product/system resolution, `MotionConfig reducedMotion=\"never\"`, and CSS media queries.\n+\n+### Controlling counts\n+\n+{count_table}\n+\n+An item can appear in multiple failure categories. Exact membership is in `Animation_System_Audit_Matrix.csv`; counts must not be summed as if mutually exclusive.\n+\n+## 2. Audit baseline and methodology\n+\n+- Authoritative application snapshot: `{BASELINE}` in the clean detached worktree under `artifacts/validation/animation-audit/baseline`.\n+- The main checkout advanced only through synchronization commits during the audit and contained substantial concurrent uncommitted work. That work was treated as out of baseline and left untouched.\n+- Static work covered registry definitions, callers, triggers, selectors, `data-scene-part`, `data-gsap-owned`, `data-motion-owned`, `data-journal-actor`, `data-journal-phase`, state machines, library wrappers, assets, CSS, audio, event mapping, replay and acknowledgment paths.\n+- Dynamic work used an isolated port 3100 only after verifying port ownership; port 3000 was never reused or stopped. Focused tests and live target measurements were retained from the original audit task.\n+- Truth rule: **build/run/complete is not visible success**. Runtime proof requires an expected trigger, correct visible instance, required target count, uniqueness, stable final semantic state, and correct persistence/acknowledgment behavior.\n+- One final mutation flow was stopped when Next.js root inference proved the detached server was using the main checkout database. Only exact rows created by the audit run were transactionally removed after a backup and safety assertions. No further mutation run was attempted without a provably isolated database path.\n+\n+## 3. Current architecture\n+\n+`AnimationProvider` exposes product mode, `AnimationDirector`, audio, metrics, and asset/runtime state. The director resolves one of 28 registry scenes and gives the builder a root-scoped selector helper. Scene builders create GSAP timelines and semantic labels; Player and Quartermaster call them from event/action handlers. Motion independently owns React presence/layout. StPageFlip clones hidden React page sources into a separate visible host. Rive and Lottie wrappers mount asset contracts. CSS data attributes and a Web Animations observer drive the journal-opening phase chain.\n+\n+The layering choices are sound in principle: GSAP for narrative sequencing, Motion for UI presence/layout, StPageFlip for physical curl, Rive for stateful authored objects, Lottie for decorative loops/one-shots, CSS for materials/ambient states, WAAPI for observing CSS completion, and Web Audio for semantic cues. The defect is coordination: target identity, ownership, reduced motion, interruption, final state, replay, and event acknowledgment are not governed by one enforceable contract.\n+\n+## 4. Current library inventory\n+\n+{library_table}\n+\n+Static CSS counts at the baseline are 7 `@keyframes`, 18 animation declarations, and 49 transition declarations. The named keyframes are `journal-breathe`, `journal-page-bow`, `drawer-in`, `runtime-arrive`, `transformation-reveal`, `journal-artifact-reveal`, and `journal-live-ink`.\n+\n+## 5. Scene reachability report\n+\n+{scene_table}\n+\n+Production-path scenes ({len(production_scenes)}): {\", \".join(f'`{name}`' for name in production_scenes)}.\n+\n+Showcase-only/dead/redundant scenes ({len(showcase_only)}): {\", \".join(f'`{name}`' for name in showcase_only)}.\n+\n+The registered names `journal-open`, `manual-page-flip`, `programmatic-page-flip`, `chapter-heading`, `prose-ink`, `marker-stamp`, `ship-course`, and `artifact-inspection` do not have a production caller at this baseline. Retire the two PageFlip scenes; retire or merge `journal-open` into the opening-machine contract; connect only the remaining names that receive a product trigger and a scoped target contract.\n+\n+## 6. Animations that do not run\n+\n+The eight scenes above run only in the development showcase. The four production Rive objects do not run as Rive at all: Invitation Seal and Finale Mechanism mount fallbacks, while Journal Clasp and Voyage Compass are contract-only. Modern `PlayerSignIn`, `StaffSignIn`, and `InvitationCeremony` do not call the legacy `player-access`/`quartermaster-login` cinematic paths. The matrix's {category_counts['broken']} broken items include these absent production paths plus infrastructure that makes successful completion unsafe.\n+\n+## 7. Animations that run invisibly\n+\n+- `parts()` returns zero-to-many nodes and no scene declares required, optional, unique, visible, or instance-scoped targets. All 28 timelines can complete with zero targets.\n+- Player event scenes query the Player root while only one section is mounted. Chapter content is absent outside Journal; Chart, Altar, Ledger, Log and Finale objects are likewise section-dependent.\n+- The temporary event overlay repeats generic part names also used by permanent sections. Static source inventory found 167 scene-part attributes but only 60 unique names. Examples: `route-path`, `map-fog`, and `artifact-reveal` each occur five times; several connection/light/stamp targets occur four times.\n+- Live landing evidence found `stars` at zero height and `arrival-copy` twice. Live journal evidence found `peripheral` twice and both visible.\n+- StPageFlip retains scene/ownership attributes in hidden React sources and cloned visible content. The tested page snapshot did not contain event-scene targets, so actual event-target duplication through PageFlip remains a structural risk, not a claimed observed occurrence.\n+\n+## 8. Animations that run poorly\n+\n+- Chapter release rendered but took 10,081 ms, over its `<10,000 ms` test contract.\n+- A wrong Quartermaster credential produced HTTP 401 but no `.form-error` within 10 seconds in the clean baseline acceptance retry.\n+- Mode changes recreate every Lottie runtime and can restart the non-looping ink bloom.\n+- Full-to-gentle does not update StPageFlip's 1100 ms timing to the intended 620 ms without remount.\n+- Director cleanup always reverts the GSAP context; route-changing success can briefly snap to its pre-scene state unless the route commits first. This is a code-path risk that still needs a visual checkpoint.\n+- Broad root queries animate duplicate nodes and do extra work. Blur/fog layers and repeated SVG path work are unbudgeted on mobile.\n+\n+## 9. One-shot and replay analysis\n+\n+Correct one-shot policies are first-arrival within a tab, an authoritative invitation acceptance, a server event's automatic ceremony, a destructive-command receipt, and a newly published immutable version. These should not mutate server state when replayed.\n+\n+Incorrect policies are state stored only in component memory/session storage when the presentation is historically meaningful. Player progression events need a persisted event-history presentation payload plus a local `presentation status` (`unseen`, `presenting`, `presented`, `skipped`, `failed`, `replay`). Replay must be available from every relevant section, must use the original payload, must not send the command again, must restore the user's section/focus, and must work after refresh. `lastRelease` fails those requirements. Lottie mode recreation is accidental replay, not a replay policy.\n+\n+## 10. Ownership conflicts\n+\n+- `claimRuntimeOwnership` rejection only warns and returns false; the director does not stop the GSAP query/timeline. Motion does not register in the WeakMap.\n+- `VoyageChart` markers combine Motion transform/layout with GSAP ownership on the same button.\n+- `ShipsLog` applies Motion layout and GSAP to the same `motion.li`.\n+- `ArtifactInspection` applies Motion `layoutId` and declares the same node GSAP-owned; the GSAP scene is dormant today but unsafe to connect directly.\n+- Companion header/navigation both expose `peripheral`; chapter release dims two visible targets.\n+- Quartermaster combines Motion UI and broad GSAP root ownership.\n+- PageFlip sources and clones retain selector attributes. The fix is not to ban a library: put each engine on a dedicated wrapper/child and enforce claims before playback.\n+\n+## 11. Wrong-library findings\n+\n+StPageFlip must exclusively own page curl, so manual/programmatic GSAP page-flip scenes should be removed. Motion should own dialogs, drawers, cards, tabs, filters, shared layout, route content and list updates. GSAP should own bounded narrative order and SVG/path choreography, not general UI presence. CSS should retain materials, hover/focus and low-cost ambience, while narrative ordering leaves CSS state chains. Rive should own the four named stateful objects after real assets exist. Lottie should remain decorative and receive semantic segment/play commands for non-looping effects. WAAPI should observe CSS completion but not become an unbounded blocking state machine.\n+\n+## 12. Lifecycle and cleanup findings\n+\n+`waitForJournalPhase()` uses `Promise.allSettled(animation.finished)` without timeout or abort racing. Missing actor or missing animation resolves immediately; an infinite animation never resolves. Abort during that wait is ineffective until all promises settle. Director context reversion lacks a declared `commit final pose` versus `revert on failure` policy. Player event errors are caught and followed by acknowledgment. PageFlip recreates under some but not all setting changes. Every runtime needs an idempotent teardown, abort priority, final-state policy, focus handoff, and test for route unmount/mode change/visibility change.\n+\n+## 13. Reduced-motion findings\n+\n+`resolveMotionMode` correctly chooses the stricter product/system preference, but `MotionConfig reducedMotion=\"never\"` prevents Motion from independently honoring the browser, and CSS still independently honors the media query. Components that do not call `useMotionMode` can diverge. Reduced showcase success proves the harness remains usable; it does not prove each product path produces the same semantic state. The target design is one resolved policy object consumed by GSAP durations/travel, Motion variants, PageFlip mode, Rive inputs, Lottie play/segments, CSS root attributes, and audio. Reduced mode must preserve order, meaning, focus and readable final state while eliminating spatial/continuous motion.\n+\n+## 14. Performance findings\n+\n+No production performance budget is enforced. Main risks are repeated root queries, duplicate target writes, Lottie recreation on every mode change, multiple fog runtimes, blur/filter animation, PageFlip clone recreation, idle CSS loops continuing while hidden, and possible listener/runtime leaks across route/replay cycles. The reduced showcase ended at approximately 161 FPS with three long tasks in one development run, but development telemetry and a reduced-only showcase are not representative production budgets. Use p95 frame time, long-task count, layout shift, runtime-instance deltas and heap/listener deltas across at least 20 replay/unmount cycles.\n+\n+## 15. Per-screen audit\n+\n+{screen_table}\n+\n+## 16. Per-library audit\n+\n+### GSAP\n+\n+Every registered scene appears in Section 5 and in matrix rows `AS-001` through `AS-028`, including current callers, selectors summarized as intended targets, replay and empty-target status. There are no required targets today. No selector is proven unique. `skip()` and completion move timeline time, not semantic application state. `reversible` exists for command concepts such as undo, but production does not expose a generalized reverse controller. Add dedicated scene hosts, event/scene instance IDs, required/optional target specs, ownership enforcement, interruption priority, final-state commit policy, semantic audio labels and runtime assertions. Retire PageFlip imitation scenes.\n+\n+### Motion\n+\n+Motion is appropriate for presence, layout, shared layout, buttons, cards, tabs, filters and navigation. The baseline uses it in Player and Quartermaster but sparsely in the modern platform. Same-node risks are enumerated in Section 10 and matrix `AM-*`. Split Motion layout/presence to outer wrappers and GSAP/Rive/Lottie visuals to inner nodes; never let both write transform/opacity on the same element.\n+\n+### StPageFlip\n+\n+Initialization, keyboard/buttons/drag, pending imperative turn, page update, page clamp, orientation event, failure/reduced fallback and cleanup have component coverage. Defects are missing full-to-gentle settings application and unsanitized clone/source attributes. Preserve page identity/current page across filter updates; test responsive single/double page behavior at all required viewports. Remove manual/programmatic GSAP curl scenes.\n+\n+### Rive\n+\n+{rive_specs}\n+\n+The only real local binary is the development sample. A fallback must be labeled as fallback in metrics and product diagnostics, never as production Rive success. Each project asset needs versioned artboard/state-machine names, validated input schema, load/stall/error timeout, deterministic static pose and screenshot checkpoints.\n+\n+### Lottie\n+\n+All three JSON files validate. Waves and fog loop/autoplay as ambient decoration and pause for element/document visibility. Ink bloom is non-looping autoplay. Because `mode` is a `useEffect` dependency, each mode change destroys/reloads/restarts the player. Remove mode from asset lifecycle where possible; update speed/segment in place. Make ink bloom `autoplay=false` and play it from the `ink` semantic label. Add stalled-load timeout, fallback metric, mode-continuity and hidden-tab tests.\n+\n+### CSS\n+\n+Keep texture, paper bow, ink/material state, hover/focus and restrained ambience in CSS. Move `drawer-in`, `runtime-arrive`, transformation ordering and other application/narrative presence into Motion or a bounded GSAP scene. Pause ambient loops when hidden. Ensure one root motion attribute disables transform/continuous motion and does not rely on media rules competing with JavaScript.\n+\n+### Web Animations API\n+\n+Validate the expected actor exists and is visible; distinguish `no animation expected` from `animation missing`; filter finite relevant animations; race completion with abort and bounded timeout; cancel owned animations on teardown; apply/log a deterministic fail-open semantic pose. Never wait on an unrelated infinite animation.\n+\n+### Audio\n+\n+Fire audio from director semantic labels only after required-target validation, not before `play()`. Map cue, offset, mute/reduced behavior and failure fallback in the scene contract. Current progression has incomplete event coverage; audio must never be the only evidence that a ceremony ran.\n+\n+## 17. Massive missing-animation list\n+\n+The baseline is missing **{len(proposals)}** planned moments across **{len(screen_counts)}** screen groups. These are implementation specifications, not claims that product state is absent. They prioritize truthful state transitions and accessibility before decoration.\n+\n+{blueprint_table}\n+\n+## 18. Recommended architecture\n+\n+1. Introduce a versioned `SceneContract`: event/action trigger, payload schema, host/instance ID, required/optional targets with visible/unique rules, libraries/property ownership, modes, labels/audio, interruption priority, timeout, final-state policy, replay policy and acknowledgment policy.\n+2. Mount one persistent Player `ProgressionSceneHost` outside section presence. It owns global event presentation and can hand settled state to a section; section components never determine whether a mandatory event can be presented.\n+3. Make `AnimationDirector.play()` return a structured receipt: target counts, visibility, duplicate/ownership errors, labels reached, mode, duration, interruption, fallback and semantic final state. Mandatory scenes cannot acknowledge viewed until the receipt meets policy or the user explicitly skips.\n+4. Persist replayable event payloads and presentation receipts. Replay never repeats a mutation and survives refresh.\n+5. Enforce wrapper-level ownership. Motion outer wrappers handle UI/layout; GSAP/Rive/Lottie/CSS child nodes handle their declared properties; StPageFlip owns curl host nodes. Strip scene ownership attributes from hidden PageFlip sources or exclude them by host/instance.\n+6. Resolve motion once at the provider and distribute it to every engine. Add project-authored Rive assets behind validated contracts and retain explicit static fallbacks.\n+\n+## 19. Severity-ranked findings\n+\n+{severity_table}\n+\n+Start with roadmap Phase 1. Do not begin decorative expansion or Rive art integration before target truth, event acknowledgment, phase interruption and replay persistence are correct.\n+\n+## 20. Evidence references\n+\n+- **E-01 - baseline/worktree:** Git status, branch/upstream and detached-worktree checks established `{BASELINE}` as the audit snapshot and kept the dirty main checkout out of baseline.\n+- **E-02 - static graph:** registry, callers, selectors, event mapping, library wrappers, CSS, assets and state/replay paths at the baseline; summarized in the matrix and scene table.\n+- **E-03 - unit/component:** 13 files and 44 tests passed across core/director/scenes/opening machine/Rive/Lottie/PageFlip/workspace interactions.\n+- **E-04 - assets:** `npm run assets:validate` passed: 3 Lottie JSON, 1 local development Rive binary, local SVG fallbacks.\n+- **E-05 - focused browser:** 3 focused animation E2E tests passed; the WebKit-mobile showcase test was skipped. Chromium and WebKit-mobile first-arrival coverage passed.\n+- **E-06 - live showcase:** all 36 entries exercised in reduced mode, no logged runtime exception; final metrics GSAP 0, Rive 1, Lottie 2, PageFlip 0, assets ready/no failures.\n+- **E-07 - landing:** six required viewports had no horizontal overflow; at 1440x900 stars had zero height and arrival-copy count was two; reduced content remained readable.\n+- **E-08 - journal/PageFlip:** live journal had ten scene-part elements and two visible peripheral targets; PageFlip clone architecture was inspected. No actual event target was observed inside the tested clone snapshot.\n+- **E-09 - authentication acceptance:** baseline wrong-login request returned 401, but `.form-error` did not appear within 10 seconds.\n+- **E-10 - chapter-release acceptance:** authoritative release reached visible seal/parchment/ink stages and exposed replay, but measured 10,081 ms and failed the `<10,000 ms` contract.\n+- **E-11 - isolation boundary:** Next root inference attached the detached server to the main database. Port 3100 was stopped, exact audit rows were removed transactionally after a backup, baseline fields/counts were verified, and no further mutation flow was claimed.\n+- Full command/output evidence is in `{TRANSCRIPT}` and its source raw transcript; generated runtime artifacts remain ignored under `artifacts/validation/animation-audit/`.\n+\n+## 21. Limitations and items requiring further runtime confirmation\n+\n+- The every-event/every-section matrix is a required future test, not a completed runtime claim. Static scoping proves section dependence; it does not identify which duplicate wins in every browser/layout.\n+- Chapter-release was not continued through refresh replay, offline interruption, gentle/reduced variants, all sections or all six viewports because the runtime attached to the wrong database. The exact observed boundary is E-10.\n+- The wrong-login feedback defect was observed, but modern and legacy auth timing should be separated in an explicitly seeded isolated environment.\n+- GSAP cleanup snapback, PageFlip event-target duplication and same-node transform outcomes are structurally demonstrated risks that still need visual/property-write instrumentation.\n+- Production Rive visual quality cannot be tested because the four project assets do not exist. Fallback behavior and the development sample are the only runtime evidence.\n+- The focused mobile showcase test was skipped by the existing suite. No claim of complete mobile showcase coverage is made.\n+- No large trace or screenshot set is committed. The matrix identifies every row whose status is `needs_runtime_verification`.\n+\"\"\"\n+write_text(DOCS / \"Animation_System_Full_Audit.md\", master)\n*** End Patch";

* Reconciling contradictory findings
* Verifying severe or uncertain claims

Otherwise declare strict path ownership before editing.\n- Designate one integration owner for shared files. Use an explicit handoff before ownership moves between agents.\n- Each implementation lane runs focused checks for its bounded work; centralized integration validation follows combination.\n- Never casually merge over, overwrite, stage, or discard another lane's or the user's changes.\n\n## Runtime and expensive-resource ownership\n\nAssign one authoritative owner for each shared or expensive resource: the development server, port `3000`, shared browser sessions, databases, migration execution, full end-to-end suites, full production builds, dependency installation, manifests and lockfiles, generated assets, deployment environments, and the final synchronization workflow.\n\nOther lanes may consume existing logs or results and request targeted checks from the owner. They must not start a competing server, mutate shared database state, regenerate shared artifacts, install dependencies, or run a duplicate full suite without a demonstrated need and safe isolation. Before acquiring a runtime, inspect recorded state and listeners; release or hand off ownership explicitly.\n\n## Testing and validation parallelization\n\n- Run safe independent unit-test groups concurrently and shard by package, subsystem, feature, or test type.\n- Let implementation lanes run focused checks for their work. Do not make every lane run the complete suite.\n- Assign one integration owner to deduplicate lint, type-check, build, browser, server, and database work and run broader cross-cutting checks after integration.\n- Run the full required acceptance gate once on the ready integrated state unless the task or failure investigation requires an additional run.\n- Preserve failure evidence and distinguish pre-existing failures from regressions introduced by the task.\n- Treat shared-database mutation suites and runtime-dependent checks as exclusive resources unless isolation is proven.\n\n## Context management\n\nThe main thread retains requirements, decisions, dependency state, ownership, integration status, unresolved questions, and final conclusions. Subagents absorb noisy searches, logs, stack traces, and repetitive evidence gathering, then return compact structured summaries. Store or reference large raw output instead of repeatedly pasting it. Summarization must not drop user requirements, unknowns, decisions, blockers, or unreviewed results.\n\n## Failure and recovery\n\nIf one lane fails, independent lanes continue. The failed lane returns partial evidence and its blocker; the coordinator preserves completed work and may retry, narrow, split, reassign, or serialize only the affected portion. Do not restart all work because one lane failed. Record downstream impact, disclose unresolved gaps, and request user intervention only when authority, information, or an external state change is genuinely required.\n\n## Completion standard\n\nDo not claim a nontrivial task complete until:\n\n- every required workstream is complete or explicitly accounted for;\n- all agent output that could materially change the result has been reviewed;\n- duplicates, contradictions, and conflicting conclusions are reconciled or disclosed;\n- shared-file integration and ownership handoffs are complete;\n- focused and required cross-cutting validation has run on the integrated state;\n- the result has been rechecked against the original request; and\n- skipped checks, known limitations, unresolved risks, and pre-existing failures are reported accurately.\n\n## Execution plans\n\nEvery substantial multi-step, cross-system, audit, refactor, migration, documentation, architecture, or implementation task must follow the structure in `PLANS.md`. The plan may remain in the task's coordination state or be written to the task's requested planning artifact. When a durable authored plan is required, place it under `Development_Docs` according to that directory's rules; root `PLANS.md` is the reusable planning standard, not a competing plan archive. Update the plan when discoveries change dependencies, ownership, safe concurrency, validation, or risk, while preserving completed work.\n\n## Shared development documentation\n\n`Development_Docs` at the repository root is the canonical shared location for development requirements, project history, architecture, decisions, implementation plans, and related authored documentation.\n\nBefore beginning work that depends on those materials:\n\n1. Confirm the repository, active branch, upstream, and complete working-tree state.\n2. Preserve any local modifications, then run `git fetch --prune origin`.\n3. Determine whether the branch is behind, ahead, or diverged with `git rev-list --left-right --count \"HEAD...@{upstream}\"`.\n4. Pull with `--ff-only`, or rebase when the repository's branch policy permits it, only when the operation is demonstrably safe. Never discard local work to synchronize.\n5. If the same document changed on two computers, preserve both sets of work and surface conflicts. Perform a normal textual merge only when clearly correct. Keep clearly differentiated variants of unmergeable binary documents when appropriate, and never push unresolved conflict markers.\n\nNever use `git reset --hard`, `git clean -fd`, `git checkout -- .`, `git restore .`, `git push --force`, or `git push --force-with-lease` as synchronization shortcuts.\n\nInspect `Development_Docs` before work when project history, requirements, architecture, decisions, or plans are relevant. Update a relevant document when an implementation decision makes it inaccurate, but do not modify unrelated documents merely because they were read. Preserve authored content and native formatting, avoid normalization churn, and do not use the directory for routine terminal output or generated test/build artifacts. Never upload secrets, credentials, private configuration, production data, or unapproved sensitive company information.\n\n## Mandatory task finalization\n\nFor every Codex task performed in this repository, conversation and development-document synchronization is a required finalization gate. Do not delete or weaken these steps:\n\n1. Complete and test the requested project work.\n2. Inspect the complete working tree and classify task changes, `Development_Docs` changes, `Codex_Chats` changes, unrelated pre-existing work, and generated files. Never stage the whole repository by default.\n3. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py --dry-run`. Review conversation classifications and the structured `development_docs` status, including eligible, excluded, suspicious, large, conflicted, renamed, and deleted paths.\n4. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py` to ingest every currently accessible project-associated Codex session and any newer export in `Codex_Chats/imports/`, and to commit eligible `Development_Docs` changes through the same path-scoped workflow.\n5. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py --validate`, inspect the exact synchronization-owned Git diff, and re-check the complete repository status.\n6. Confirm each command's exit status and structured report. Never claim a conversation was archived unless a source was read and a transcript was written successfully. Never claim documentation was synchronized unless the reported commit and push state prove it.\n7. If synchronization content changed, confirm the scoped commit and permitted push succeeded and verify the remote SHA. If nothing changed, confirm no empty commit was created. Report unrelated remaining changes accurately.\n8. Include concise `Chat archive:` and `Development docs:` result lines in the final response.\n\nThe synchronizer is deliberately not a `post-commit` hook because many prompts finish without a code commit. The Codex host currently exposes no repository task-completion hook that can be versioned here, so this instruction is authoritative. A task's final assistant message can only be captured on the next synchronization because it does not exist until after the pre-finalization run; the next task must update that same stable conversation rather than creating a duplicate.\n\n`Development_Docs…24 tokens truncated…can begin immediately?\n- Which steps must be serialized, and for what concrete reason?\n- Which resources are shared or exclusive?\n- Which files or interfaces are conflict-prone?\n- Which work should be read-only?\n- Which write lanes need isolated worktrees?\n- What is the maximum safe and useful concurrency under current capacity?\n- Why does the chosen decomposition improve speed, coverage, reliability, or context management without redundant work?\n- What conditions would cause a lane to be split, collapsed, cancelled, or serialized?\n\nFor a substantial task that remains primarily sequential, document the dependency, safety, exclusive-resource, or overlapping-file reason. Ease of coordination is not enough.\n\n### 8. Dependency graph\n\nRepresent lane identifiers, prerequisites, fan-out, fan-in, the critical path, and failure independence. A compact text or Mermaid graph is acceptable. For example:\n\n```text\nP0 repository preflight\n|-- R1 read-only subsystem inventory --\\\n|-- R2 read-only test/risk inventory ----+-- I1 coordinator reconciliation\n`-- R3 read-only requirements review ---/\n                                         |-- W1 isolated implementation lane --\\\n                                         `-- W2 isolated implementation lane --+-- I2 shared-file integration\n                                                                                 `-- V1 integrated validation\n                                                                                     `-- F1 finalization\n```\n\nAnnotate:\n\n- prerequisites and blocking relationships;\n- ready work and fan-out points;\n- integration/fan-in points;\n- the critical path;\n- serialized shared-resource operations; and\n- lanes that can continue if another lane fails.\n\nDo not force parallel work into a fake sequential checklist.\n\n### 9. Workstream matrix\n\n| Lane ID | Lane name | Objective | Agent role | Read/write | Owned scope | Owned paths | Explicit non-scope | Dependencies | Shared resources | Deliverables | Required evidence | Focused validation | Status | Blockers |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| R1 |  |  |  | Read-only |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  | Ready |  |\n\nEvery lane must have a non-overlapping evidence or edit boundary. If two write lanes need the same file, change the ownership or serialize the handoff before either edits.\n\n### 10. Subagent briefing template\n\nUse a bounded briefing such as:\n\n```text\nObjective:\nBackground and governing requirements:\nScope:\nExplicit non-scope:\nAuthorization: read-only | write\nOwned concerns/paths:\nResources and paths you must not modify:\nDependencies and prerequisite evidence:\nConstraints and invariants:\nRequired repository-wide searches or checks:\nRequired deliverable:\nRequired evidence (paths, symbols, commands, reproduction, confidence):\nFocused validation:\nReturn format (compact summary, changes, evidence, tests, blockers):\nCompletion criteria:\nStop conditions:\nBlocker/uncertainty/ownership-conflict reporting:\n```\n\nBriefings must carry the requirements the worker needs; do not rely on the worker to reconstruct user intent. Require distilled results, not full raw logs. A worker must stop and report if its scope becomes ambiguous, overlaps another writer, requires a prohibited shared resource, or would broaden the task.\n\n### 11. Shared-resource and locking plan\n\n| Resource | Authoritative owner | Other permitted consumers | Exclusive operations | Conflict-prevention method | Release/handoff condition |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Port `3000` and development server |  | Existing logs/HTTP evidence only | Start/stop/restart | Inspect listener and recorded launcher state; one owner | Server stopped or ownership acknowledged |\n| Browser session |  | Read-only observers if safe | Stateful automation | Separate contexts or one controller | State/evidence saved |\n| Database and seed state |  | Read-only queries if explicitly safe | Mutation/reset/seed/migration | One database owner; back up when required | State and evidence recorded |\n| Prisma schemas and migrations |  | Read-only review | Schema edit, migration creation/application | One authoritative writer; preserve order | Schema/migration validated and handed off |\n| `package.json` / `package-lock.json` |  | Read-only review | Dependency install/regeneration | One package owner | Lockfile diff reviewed |\n| Global styles / layout / providers |  | Read-only review | Shared interface edits | One integration owner | Consumers reconciled |\n| Route definitions |  | Read-only inventory | Conflicting route/layout edits | Path owner plus integration review | Route map validated |\n| Generated assets |  | Consumers of existing output | Regeneration | One generator, deterministic inputs | Output and provenance reviewed |\n| Full E2E/build/validation |  | Consumers of reports | Expensive full run | One validation owner | Results saved and reviewed |\n| Deployment environment |  | Read-only status consumers | Deploy/rollback/config mutation | Explicit authority and one operator | Health and rollback state confirmed |\n| Chat/document synchronization | Coordinator | Workers provide summaries only | Dry-run/live/validate/commit/push | Run once after lanes finish | Structured report and remote state verified |\n\nAdd task-specific resources. In this repository, normal development uses `npm run dev:full` on `127.0.0.1:3000`. The Playwright default test port (`3100`) and validation production proof port (`3200`) are isolated validation resources, not extra canonical app instances.\n\n### 12. File ownership plan\n\nRecord:\n\n- one writer per file at a time;\n- path/component/package boundaries for every write lane;\n- the integration owner for shared files;\n- read-only reviewers assigned where edit overlap would otherwise occur;\n- worktree locations or isolation method for independent implementation lanes;\n- pre-existing files that no lane may stage, overwrite, or normalize;\n- ownership handoff order and evidence; and\n- the rule for newly discovered shared files.\n\nRecommended table:\n\n| Path or pattern | Writer/owner | Read-only reviewers | Ownership window | Dependencies | Handoff condition |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n|  |  |  |  |  |  |\n\nPackage manifests and lockfiles, Prisma schemas/migrations/seed, root layouts and providers, global styles/tokens, route entrypoints, central registries, runtime configuration, and generated files are presumed conflict-prone until inspected. Exactly one integration lane owns each such resource.\n\n### 13. Runtime strategy\n\nDefine:\n\n- who may start and stop the application;\n- the exact verified command;\n- how canonical port `3000` ownership is checked and maintained;\n- whether an already-running instance can be reused;\n- how other lanes request runtime evidence without starting duplicates;\n- how browser testing is divided and state isolated;\n- where server logs and screenshots are stored or referenced;\n- how stale or unknown processes are detected and handled safely; and\n- when runtime validation occurs relative to integration.\n\nRepository defaults:\n\n- use `npm run dev:full` and `npm run dev:stop` for the normal development lifecycle;\n- never kill an unknown listener merely to acquire port `3000`;\n- do not let workers run competing servers;\n- use Playwright's configured isolated server for E2E ownership, not the shared development server, unless the plan explicitly proves a safe alternative;\n- treat database-mutating browser work as exclusive unless separate databases are configured; and\n- centralize final browser/runtime review after integration.\n\n### 14. Testing strategy\n\nPlan all applicable layers:\n\n| Layer | Owner | Focused or integrated | Parallelizable shards | Serialized resource | Required evidence | Pass/fail treatment |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Format/lint/type |  |  |  |  |  |  |\n| Unit/component |  |  |  |  |  |  |\n| Asset/contracts |  |  |  |  |  |  |\n| Integration/API |  |  |  | Database/runtime if used |  |  |\n| E2E/accessibility |  |  |  | Server/browser/database |  |  |\n| Production build |  | Integrated | No duplicate full builds | Build cache/output |  |  |\n| Performance |  |  |  | Target runtime |  |  |\n| Regression/acceptance |  | Integrated |  | Full gate |  |  |\n\nUse only verified commands. Available focused commands include `npm run format:check`, `npm run lint`, `npm run typecheck`, `npm test`, `npm run assets:validate`, `npm run test:e2e`, and `npm run build`. `npm run validate` is the full cross-cutting gate. No separate `test:integration` script is currently verified; describe the actual focused Vitest/Playwright coverage instead of inventing one.\n\nImplementation lanes run the smallest relevant checks. Independent unit shards may run concurrently. One validation owner deduplicates full lint/type/build/browser/server work and runs the required integrated gate on the combined state. Record commands, exit status, relevant counts, artifact paths, and failures. Classify failures as pre-existing, task regression, environmental, or unresolved with evidence; never erase failure evidence to make a gate appear clean.\n\n### 15. Integration strategy\n\nDefine:\n\n- integration owner;\n- lane handoff and merge order based on dependencies;\n- shared-file update owner and timing;\n- conflict-resolution process;\n- how duplicated or competing changes are detected;\n- how cross-lane assumptions and interfaces are checked;\n- how the complete integrated diff and working tree are reviewed; and\n- focused and cross-cutting checks to run after integration.\n\nThe integration owner preserves both sides of unexpected conflicts, consults the governing source, and resolves only when intent is clear. Workers do not casually merge, stage, or overwrite one another's work. Final Git reconciliation, if authorized, remains serialized.\n\n### 16. Risk register\n\n| Risk ID | Description | Probability | Impact | Detection method | Mitigation | Owner | Recovery action | Status |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| K1 |  | Low/Med/High | Low/Med/High |  |  |  |  | Open |\n\nInclude ownership collision, stale repository assumptions, accidental scope expansion, runtime/port/database contention, migration ordering, generated-file churn, pre-existing failures, security/privacy exposure, incomplete integration, and validation gaps where applicable.\n\n### 17. Decision log\n\n| Decision ID | Decision | Reason | Alternatives considered | Evidence | Affected lanes | Date/stage |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| D1 |  |  |  |  |  |  |\n\nRecord concise rationale and evidence, not private chain-of-thought.\n\n### 18. Discovery log\n\n| Discovery ID | Finding | Evidence | Changes scope/dependencies/ownership/validation/risk | Plan update | Date/stage |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| X1 |  |  |  |  |  |\n\nLog discoveries that materially change architecture assumptions, canonical locations, dependency edges, ownership, validation, risk, or completion criteria.\n\n### 19. Progress tracking\n\nUse these statuses consistently:\n\n- **Not ready** - prerequisites are unresolved.\n- **Ready** - prerequisites are satisfied and ownership is available.\n- **Active** - work is currently running.\n- **Blocked** - a specific blocker prevents progress.\n- **Awaiting integration** - lane deliverable is complete but not yet reconciled.\n- **Integrated** - combined into the coordinator's working state.\n- **Verified** - required focused and integrated checks passed or were accounted for.\n- **Complete** - acceptance criteria and reporting are finished.\n- **Cancelled as redundant** - lane is no longer necessary and useful partial evidence is preserved.\n\nPlans reflect actual state, not optimistic expectations. At most one owner writes a file or holds an exclusive resource at a time.\n\n### 20. Failure recovery\n\nFor each material lane or shared resource, define:\n\n- how partial results are preserved;\n- retry limit or retry condition;\n- when to narrow, split, or reassign a lane;\n- when an initially parallel lane must be serialized;\n- which dependent lanes become blocked;\n- which unaffected lanes continue;\n- how ownership and runtime locks are released;\n- recovery or rollback action for mutations; and\n- when user intervention is genuinely required.\n\nDo not restart completed work because another lane failed. A failed lane returns partial evidence and a precise blocker. The coordinator discloses any gap that remains material to completion.\n\n### 21. Final verification\n\nBefore claiming completion:\n\n- re-read the original user request and governing sources;\n- map every acceptance criterion to a delivered change or verified result;\n- reconcile every lane output and resolve or disclose contradictions;\n- confirm no material agent result remains unreviewed;\n- inspect the complete integrated diff and working tree for accidental scope expansion;\n- verify shared-file ownership and handoffs are complete;\n- run required focused, integrated, runtime, regression, accessibility, performance, security, migration, build, or acceptance checks as applicable;\n- distinguish skipped, unverified, pre-existing, and failed checks;\n- confirm generated artifacts and unrelated work were not accidentally staged or treated as source; and\n- run the single coordinator-owned repository finalization gate from `AGENTS.md`.\n\nFor documentation-only work, use proportionate document validation and do not launch the application or full test suite unless a governing instruction explicitly requires it.\n\n### 22. Final report format\n\nThe coordinator reports:\n\n```text\nOutcome: what was completed\nParallel execution: lanes used and why the decomposition was safe/useful\nAffected files/systems: task-authored changes, sync-owned changes, and pre-existing work classified separately\nValidation: commands/checks, exit status, and material evidence\nFailures/limitations: unresolved or skipped checks and why\nRemaining risks: known risks that remain within or outside scope\nFollow-up: only work genuinely outside the requested scope\nChat archive: required synchronization result\nDevelopment docs: required synchronization result\n```\n\nThe report provides concise rationale, evidence, decisions, and results. It does not expose private chain-of-thought or paste raw agent logs.\n\n## Standard parallel workflow patterns\n\nAdapt these patterns to the task rather than copying them mechanically.\n\n### Repository-wide audit\n\n1. Coordinator preserves the audit prompt, taxonomy, severity definitions, evidence rules, and report format.\n2. Fan out read-only lanes by subsystem and/or distinct concern.\n3. Give each lane a bounded evidence surface and require paths, symbols, reproduction evidence, and confidence.\n4. Use a dedicated verification lane or coordinator pass for severe, disputed, or uncertain findings.\n5. Fan in through centralized deduplication, severity reconciliation, and contradiction resolution.\n6. Produce one coherent audit; do not paste lane reports together.\n\n### Multi-feature implementation\n\n1. Establish shared architecture/contracts and identify shared files.\n2. Place genuinely independent feature lanes in separate worktrees or assign disjoint paths.\n3. Give shared architecture, schemas, configuration, manifests, and lockfiles one owner.\n4. Let each feature lane run focused tests.\n5. Integrate in dependency order under one owner.\n6. Run final full-system verification once on the combined state.\n\n### Bug investigation\n\nFan out read-only lanes for reproduction, code-path tracing, recent-change/regression analysis, and test-gap analysis. Keep hypotheses distinct and evidence-backed. The coordinator reconciles them and selects a supported root cause before authorizing implementation. Parallel fixes for the same suspected defect are normally redundant unless intentionally comparing isolated approaches.\n\n### Refactor\n\nStart architecture mapping, consumer inventory, and test-baseline lanes in parallel. Serialize shared-interface changes under one owner, then fan out independent package/component migrations with disjoint ownership. Integrate in dependency order and run compatibility/regression verification after all consumers move.\n\n### Frontend experience work\n\nUse bounded component lanes plus independent design-system/visual-consistency, animation/interaction, accessibility, and performance review lanes when separable. Assign one owner to root layout, providers, shared tokens/global styles, and route shells. Assign one runtime/browser owner. Finish with an integrated responsive, keyboard, motion-mode, console, performance, and UX review against the original acceptance criteria.\n\n### Backend or database work\n\nSeparate API, repository/service, schema-analysis, test, and operational-readiness lanes where their evidence or files do not overlap. Give schemas and the ordered migration chain one authoritative writer and one database/migration operator. Serialize migration generation/application and shared state mutation. Integrate API/service contracts before final database, security, regression, and deployment-readiness validation.\n\n### Documentation task\n\nFan out read-only source discovery and fact-checking by topic. Topic-specific drafting may run in parallel only with disjoint document ownership. Use one editorial integration owner for shared documents, terminology, links, and final formatting. Verify claims against sources and run diff/format/link checks proportionate to the task. Do not create extra helper documents when the requested output is fixed.\n\n## Anti-patterns\n\nDo not:\n\n- process independent areas sequentially without a concrete reason;\n- spawn agents with substantially identical scopes;\n- make every agent scan the entire repository;\n- launch work before evidence, path, and resource ownership are defined;\n- allow multiple agents to edit the same file concurrently;\n- let multiple development servers compete for port `3000`;\n- make every lane run the entire test suite;\n- use agent count as a quality metric or create microscopic artificial lanes;\n- ignore slower-lane results because faster lanes finished first;\n- combine reports without reconciling duplicates, severity, or contradictions;\n- let subagents reinterpret or broaden user requirements;\n- use parallelism to bypass safety, review, authorization, or validation;\n- restart completed work after one lane fails;\n- claim completion before integration and verification;\n- create alternate application copies, port-specific versions, or abandoned variants;\n- use generated snapshots or obsolete/source-history trees because they are easier to discover;\n- hide uncertainty, partial failure, skipped validation, or incomplete integration;\n- let workers independently fetch/rebase/merge/commit/push or run chat/document synchronization without explicit exclusive ownership; or\n- stage the whole repository by default in a dirty worktree.\n\n## Adaptive plan behavior\n\nUpdate the plan when:\n\n- new dependencies or shared resources are discovered;\n- two lanes overlap or shared-file conflict becomes likely;\n- a lane becomes unnecessary or duplicates another;\n- supposedly independent work is found to be dependent;\n- a blocked lane can be narrowed or split;\n- additional safe parallelism becomes available;\n- runtime evidence contradicts static analysis;\n- the canonical implementation differs from the initial assumption;\n- validation reveals a pre-existing failure or regression; or\n- risk, completion criteria, or user authority changes.\n\nWhen adapting:\n\n1. preserve completed work and useful partial evidence;\n2. update dependency edges, ownership, locks, status, risk, and validation;\n3. notify affected lanes and stop conflicting writers before reassignment;\n4. record the material discovery or decision;\n5. start newly ready independent work promptly; and\n6. avoid unnecessary restarts.\n\nThe plan serves the task. It may evolve, but it may never override the user's instructions or governing product requirements.\n\r\n--- NESTED INSTRUCTION FILES ---\r\n.\\AGENTS.md\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]
