Do not modify or cite these as canonical source. In particular, `artifacts/validation/animation-audit/baseline/AGENTS.md` is an ignored generated snapshot, not an active nested instruction file.\r\n26: \r\n27: Verify the canonical implementation before broad exploration or modification. Do not create port-specific application copies, abandoned local variants, duplicate runtimes, or parallel implementations of existing canonical surfaces.\r\n28: \r\n29: ### Verified commands and ports\r\n30: \r\n31: | Purpose | Verified command or behavior |\r\n32: | --- | --- |\r\n33: | Canonical development start | `npm run dev:full` |\r\n34: | Canonical development stop | `npm run dev:stop` |\r\n35: | Canonical development address | `http://127.0.0.1:3000`; port `3000` has one owner |\r\n36: | Next-only development after setup | `npm run dev` |\r\n37: | Production build | `npm run build` |\r\n38: | Lint | `npm run lint` |\r\n39: | Format check | `npm run format:check` |\r\n40: | Strict type check | `npm run typecheck` |\r\n41: | Unit/component tests | `npm test` |\r\n42: | End-to-end tests | `npm run test:e2e` |\r\n43: | Animation asset contracts | `npm run assets:validate` |\r\n44: | Full repository validation | `npm run validate` |\r\n45: \r\n46: There is no separate verified `test:integration` script; integration behavior is covered by the focused Vitest/Playwright checks and the full `npm run validate` gate. Playwright normally owns an isolated server on port `3100`, and full validation normally uses port `3200` for production restart proof. Those are controlled validation resources, not authorization to start competing development servers. An explicitly isolated alternate development port is an exception for a demonstrated need, not a new application copy.\r\n47: \r\n48: ## Parallelization-first default\r\n49: \r\n50: Every current or future nontrivial task begins automatically with a parallelization assessment using `PLANS.md`. Before work starts, identify independent workstreams, dependency edges, shared resources, likely file conflicts, immediately ready lanes, serialized gates, the coordinator, and bounded specialist ownership. Start all ready independent lanes concurrently, and keep dependent lanes blocked until prerequisites are satisfied. Record the decomposition and concrete reasons for serialization in the execution plan for substantial tasks.\r\n51: \r\n52: This repository policy is standing authorization to use subagents whenever they improve speed, coverage, reliability, or context management. The user does not need to say \"use subagents,\" \"run this in parallel,\" or \"spawn agents.\" Use the greatest safe and useful concurrency available while respecting host limits, ownership, and task requirements.\r\n53: \r\n54: ## Mandatory parallelization triggers\r\n55: \r\n56: Use parallel subagents when a task contains two or more meaningfully independent areas, including:\r\n57: \r\n58: - multiple packages, services, subsystems, routes, pages, feature domains, or acceptance criteria;\r\n59: - repository-wide audits, large codebase discovery, independent bug hypotheses, or multiple evidence sources;\r\n60: - multiple animation libraries, renderers, or independent review categories;\r\n61: - separable frontend, backend, database, API, deployment, documentation, test, accessibility, security, performance, functional, or maintainability concerns;\r\n62: - broad refactors or implementations with non-overlapping ownership boundaries;\r\n63: - independent test suites or validation surfaces; or\r\n64: - noisy exploration that would displace requirements and integration state from the coordinator's context.\r\n65: \r\n66: Broad read-heavy work strongly favors parallel specialist lanes.\r\n67: \r\n68: ## Work that normally remains serialized\r\n69: \r\n70: Keep work sequential when delegation overhead exceeds the work or when ordering or exclusive ownership is material, including:\r\n71: \r\n72: - tiny localized tasks;\r\n73: - strictly dependent steps;\r\n74: - changes concentrated in the same small group of files;\r\n75: - ordered migration chains;\r\n76: - package-manager, manifest, or lockfile updates with one authoritative writer;\r\n77: - shared schema, configuration, entrypoint, route registry, global style, or central provider changes;\r\n78: - destructive operations, production deployment, final integration, and final merges;\r\n79: - one exclusive runtime, browser, database, or deployment resource; or\r\n80: - any situation where parallel writes would create a race or ambiguous ownership.\r\n81: \r\n82: For a substantial task performed primarily sequentially, record the concrete dependency, safety, resource, or conflict reason. Coordinator convenience alone is not sufficient.\r\n83: \r\n84: ## Coordinator responsibilities\r\n85: \r\n86: The main agent is the coordinator. It must:\r\n87: \r\n88: - retain the complete user request and governing constraints;\r\n89: - perform or own repository/Git preflight, decompose the dependency graph, assign non-overlapping scopes, and prevent duplicate work;\r\n90: - give every lane a bounded objective, authorization, ownership boundary, evidence requirement, validation requirement, completion condition, and return format;\r\n91: - track dependencies and shared resources while keeping raw logs and noisy exploration out of the main context;\r\n92: - collect concise evidence-backed summaries, reconcile disagreements, and directly verify severe, risky, or uncertain findings;\r\n93: - integrate in dependency order, review the combined diff, run cross-cutting validation, and check the result against the original request;\r\n94: - produce one coherent final result rather than concatenating worker reports; and\r\n95: - own the single mandatory `Codex_Chats` / `Development_Docs` finalization workflow unless it explicitly delegates that exclusive responsibility.\r\n96: \r\n97: Do not redo delegated work except for necessary verification. Workers must not fetch, pull, rebase, merge, commit, push, or run the synchronization workflow unless the coordinator explicitly grants that bounded authority.\r\n98: \r\n99: ## Subagent contract\r\n100: \r\n101: Every delegated lane must state:\r\n102: \r\n103: - precise objective and background;\r\n104: - explicit scope and non-scope;\r\n105: - read-only or write authorization;\r\n106: - owned files, directories, components, or concerns;\r\n107: - dependencies and shared resources it may not modify;\r\n108: - required searches, evidence, and validation;\r\n109: - required deliverable and compact return format;\r\n110: - completion and stop conditions; and\r\n--- AGENTS 111-208 ---\r\n111: - instructions to report blockers, partial results, uncertainty, and ownership conflicts honestly.\r\n112: \r\n113: Subagents must not silently broaden scope. They return distilled findings or implementation summaries with relevant paths, symbols, commands, reproduction evidence, and confidence, not unfiltered command logs.\r\n114: \r\n115: ## Concurrency policy\r\n116: \r\n117: - Launch all ready independent lanes together; do not wait for one independent lane before starting another.\r\n118: - Prefer several meaningful workstreams to dozens of microscopic agents. Adjust concurrency to the dependency graph, resource capacity, and conflict risk; no fixed agent count applies.\r\n119: - Reuse coordinator discovery. Bound each lane to its own evidence or ownership area instead of making every worker scan the whole repository.\r\n120: - Avoid redundant searches, duplicated expensive operations, and artificial lanes created only to raise agent count.\r\n121: - Add lanes dynamically when new independent work appears. Collapse or cancel lanes that become redundant, and preserve useful partial results.\r\n122: \r\n123: ## Read-heavy workflow policy\r\n124: \r\n125: Audits, reviews, discovery, research, mapping, and diagnostics default to parallel read-only specialist lanes divided by subsystem, concern, library, package, route group, risk category, or evidence source. Use repository-wide search before deep file inspection and give every lane a distinct evidence boundary. A verification lane or the coordinator must recheck severe or disputed findings. Reports distinguish confirmed findings from suspicions and include paths, symbols, reproduction steps, evidence, and confidence where applicable.\r\n126: \r\n127: Read-only lanes may inspect a shared file concurrently but may not mutate Git state, runtime state, databases, generated output, or external systems without explicit authorization.\r\n128: \r\n129: ## Write-heavy workflow policy\r\n130: \r\n131: - Partition implementation by non-overlapping files, components, packages, or services. Exactly one agent may write a file at a time.\r\n132: - Use separate Codex worktrees for independent implementation chats or lanes when supported and appropriate. Otherwise declare strict path ownership before editing.\r\n133: - Designate one integration owner for shared files. Lockfiles, manifests, central configuration, schemas, migrations, generated artifacts, global styles, providers, route definitions, and entrypoints each have one authoritative lane.\r\n134: - Sequence dependent changes through the plan's dependency graph. Use an explicit handoff before ownership moves between agents.\r\n135: - Each implementation lane runs focused checks for its bounded work; centralized integration validation follows combination.\r\n136: - Never casually merge over, overwrite, stage, or discard another lane's or the user's changes.\r\n137: \r\n138: ## Runtime and expensive-resource ownership\r\n139: \r\n140: Assign one authoritative owner for each shared or expensive resource: the development server, port `3000`, shared browser sessions, databases, migration execution, full end-to-end suites, full production builds, dependency installation, manifests and lockfiles, generated assets, deployment environments, and the final synchronization workflow.\r\n141: \r\n142: Other lanes may consume existing logs or results and request targeted checks from the owner. They must not start a competing server, mutate shared database state, regenerate shared artifacts, install dependencies, or run a duplicate full suite without a demonstrated need and safe isolation. Before acquiring a runtime, inspect recorded state and listeners; release or hand off ownership explicitly.\r\n143: \r\n144: ## Testing and validation parallelization\r\n145: \r\n146: - Run safe independent unit-test groups concurrently and shard by package, subsystem, feature, or test type.\r\n147: - Let implementation lanes run focused checks for their work. Do not make every lane run the complete suite.\r\n148: - Assign one integration owner to deduplicate lint, type-check, build, browser, server, and database work and run broader cross-cutting checks after integration.\r\n149: - Run the full required acceptance gate once on the ready integrated state unless the task or failure investigation requires an additional run.\r\n150: - Preserve failure evidence and distinguish pre-existing failures from regressions introduced by the task.\r\n151: - Treat shared-database mutation suites and runtime-dependent checks as exclusive resources unless isolation is proven.\r\n152: \r\n153: ## Context management\r\n154: \r\n155: The main thread retains requirements, decisions, dependency state, ownership, integration status, unresolved questions, and final conclusions. Subagents absorb noisy searches, logs, stack traces, and repetitive evidence gathering, then return compact structured summaries. Store or reference large raw output instead of repeatedly pasting it. Summarization must not drop user requirements, unknowns, decisions, blockers, or unreviewed results.\r\n156: \r\n157: ## Failure and recovery\r\n158: \r\n159: If one lane fails, independent lanes continue. The failed lane returns partial evidence and its blocker; the coordinator preserves completed work and may retry, narrow, split, reassign, or serialize only the affected portion. Do not restart all work because one lane failed. Record downstream impact, disclose unresolved gaps, and request user intervention only when authority, information, or an external state change is genuinely required.\r\n160: \r\n161: ## Completion standard\r\n162: \r\n163: Do not claim a nontrivial task complete until:\r\n164: \r\n165: - every required workstream is complete or explicitly accounted for;\r\n166: - all agent output that could materially change the result has been reviewed;\r\n167: - duplicates, contradictions, and conflicting conclusions are reconciled or disclosed;\r\n168: - shared-file integration and ownership handoffs are complete;\r\n169: - focused and required cross-cutting validation has run on the integrated state;\r\n170: - the result has been rechecked against the original request; and\r\n171: - skipped checks, known limitations, unresolved risks, and pre-existing failures are reported accurately.\r\n172: \r\n173: ## Execution plans\r\n174: \r\n175: Every substantial multi-step, cross-system, audit, refactor, migration, documentation, architecture, or implementation task must follow the structure in `PLANS.md`. The plan may remain in the task's coordination state or be written to the task's requested planning artifact. When a durable authored plan is required, place it under `Development_Docs` according to that directory's rules; root `PLANS.md` is the reusable planning standard, not a competing plan archive. Update the plan when discoveries change dependencies, ownership, safe concurrency, validation, or risk, while preserving completed work.\r\n176: \r\n177: ## Shared development documentation\r\n178: \r\n179: `Development_Docs` at the repository root is the canonical shared location for development requirements, project history, architecture, decisions, implementation plans, and related authored documentation.\r\n180: \r\n181: Before beginning work that depends on those materials:\r\n182: \r\n183: 1. Confirm the repository, active branch, upstream, and complete working-tree state.\r\n184: 2. Preserve any local modifications, then run `git fetch --prune origin`.\r\n185: 3. Determine whether the branch is behind, ahead, or diverged with `git rev-list --left-right --count \"HEAD...@{upstream}\"`.\r\n186: 4. Pull with `--ff-only`, or rebase when the repository's branch policy permits it, only when the operation is demonstrably safe. Never discard local work to synchronize.\r\n187: 5. If the same document changed on two computers, preserve both sets of work and surface conflicts. Perform a normal textual merge only when clearly correct. Keep clearly differentiated variants of unmergeable binary documents when appropriate, and never push unresolved conflict markers.\r\n188: \r\n189: Never use `git reset --hard`, `git clean -fd`, `git checkout -- .`, `git restore .`, `git push --force`, or `git push --force-with-lease` as synchronization shortcuts.\r\n190: \r\n191: Inspect `Development_Docs` before work when project history, requirements, architecture, decisions, or plans are relevant. Update a relevant document when an implementation decision makes it inaccurate, but do not modify unrelated documents merely because they were read. Preserve authored content and native formatting, avoid normalization churn, and do not use the directory for routine terminal output or generated test/build artifacts. Never upload secrets, credentials, private configuration, production data, or unapproved sensitive company information.\r\n192: \r\n193: ## Mandatory task finalization\r\n194: \r\n195: For every Codex task performed in this repository, conversation and development-document synchronization is a required finalization gate. Do not delete or weaken these steps:\r\n196: \r\n197: 1. Complete and test the requested project work.\r\n198: 2. Inspect the complete working tree and classify task changes, `Development_Docs` changes, `Codex_Chats` changes, unrelated pre-existing work, and generated files. Never stage the whole repository by default.\r\n199: 3. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py --dry-run`. Review conversation classifications and the structured `development_docs` status, including eligible, excluded, suspicious, large, conflicted, renamed, and deleted paths.\r\n200: 4. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py` to ingest every currently accessible project-associated Codex session and any newer export in `Codex_Chats/imports/`, and to commit eligible `Development_Docs` changes through the same path-scoped workflow.\r\n201: 5. Run `python scripts/sync_codex_chats.py --validate`, inspect the exact synchronization-owned Git diff, and re-check the complete repository status.\r\n202: 6. Confirm each command's exit status and structured report. Never claim a conversation was archived unless a source was read and a transcript was written successfully. Never claim documentation was synchronized unless the reported commit and push state prove it.\r\n203: 7. If synchronization content changed, confirm the scoped commit and permitted push succeeded and verify the remote SHA. If nothing changed, confirm no empty commit was created. Report unrelated remaining changes accurately.\r\n204: 8. Include concise `Chat archive:` and `Development docs:` result lines in the final response.\r\n205: \r\n206: The synchronizer is deliberately not a `post-commit` hook because many prompts finish without a code commit. The Codex host currently exposes no repository task-completion hook that can be versioned here, so this instruction is authoritative. A task's final assistant message can only be captured on the next synchronization because it does not exist until after the pre-finalization run; the next task must update that same stable conversation rather than creating a duplicate.\r\n207: \r\n208: `Development_Docs` remains a normal Git-tracked directory. Do not copy it into `Codex_Chats`, archive it as transcripts, or maintain a duplicate mirror. The synchronizer may commit only explicit eligible paths; it must leave ignored, suspicious, oversized, conflicted, and unrelated files untouched. Do not commit raw ChatGPT exports or bypass a secret warning, unsafe Git state, ambiguous classification, source failure, integrity failure, branch divergence, or protected-branch policy.\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

It is a reusable execution standard, not a plan for one feature or audit. `AGENTS.md` supplies the governing behavior and instruction hierarchy; current user instructions and governing product documents continue to define scope and desired results.\r\n4: \r\n5: Root `PLANS.md` does not replace `Development_Docs`. When a task requires a durable authored plan or implementation record, store that task-specific artifact under `Development_Docs` in accordance with its rules. A plan may otherwise remain in the task's coordination state or in the user-requested artifact.\r\n6: \r\n7: ## When an execution plan is required\r\n8: \r\n9: Use this planning structure for work that is any of the following:\r\n10: \r\n11: - multi-step, cross-cutting, ambiguous, long-running, repository-wide, or high-risk;\r\n12: - spread across multiple subsystems or suitable for multiple subagents;\r\n13: - dependent on staged validation or likely to modify shared infrastructure;\r\n14: - an audit, feature, refactor, bug investigation, test initiative, documentation effort, architecture change, data migration, deployment preparation, performance effort, accessibility effort, or cross-system project; or\r\n15: - large enough that context loss, duplicated work, ownership ambiguity, or accidental restart is plausible.\r\n16: \r\n17: A trivial localized task may use a lightweight inline plan, but it must still respect ownership, repository safety, and finalization requirements.\r\n18: \r\n19: ## Planning principles\r\n20: \r\n21: An execution plan must:\r\n22: \r\n23: - be self-contained enough for another coordinator to continue without rediscovering the task;\r\n24: - preserve the exact user goal, terminology, constraints, acceptance criteria, methodology, and output format;\r\n25: - separate requirements and invariants from implementation choices;\r\n26: - identify verified facts, assumptions, unknowns, and canonical sources;\r\n27: - represent work as a dependency graph rather than a falsely linear checklist;\r\n28: - maximize safe, useful concurrency while identifying conflict-prone and serialized work;\r\n29: - establish one coordinator, clear lane ownership, and one writer per file at a time;\r\n30: - identify shared runtime and expensive resources with authoritative owners;\r\n31: - define evidence, focused validation, integration validation, and completion criteria before implementation;\r\n32: - remain updateable as discoveries change scope, dependencies, ownership, risk, or validation;\r\n33: - record significant decisions and discoveries without exposing private chain-of-thought; and\r\n34: - preserve completed work when the plan changes.\r\n35: \r\n36: ## Required execution-plan template\r\n37: \r\n38: Use the following sections. \"Not applicable\" is acceptable only with a short reason.\r\n39: \r\n40: ### 1. Task identity\r\n41: \r\n42: ```text\r\n43: Task title:\r\n44: Date:\r\n45: Coordinator:\r\n46: Repository and root:\r\n47: Current branch/worktree and commit:\r\n48: Governing documents:\r\n49: Related issue, phase, audit, or feature:\r\n50: Current task instructions:\r\n51: Plan location/status:\r\n52: ```\r\n53: \r\n54: Copy or faithfully summarize the controlling task instructions. Link to the full source when a summary could lose detail.\r\n55: \r\n56: ### 2. Objective\r\n57: \r\n58: Record:\r\n59: \r\n60: - desired end state;\r\n61: - user-visible outcome;\r\n62: - technical outcome; and\r\n63: - why the work is being done.\r\n64: \r\n65: Use outcome language. Do not describe activity alone as success.\r\n66: \r\n67: ### 3. Scope\r\n68: \r\n69: Record:\r\n70: \r\n71: - included work;\r\n72: - explicit exclusions;\r\n73: - areas and behaviors that must remain unchanged;\r\n74: - existing completed or uncommitted work that must be preserved; and\r\n75: - actions that require separate user authority.\r\n76: \r\n77: Parallelization never broadens this scope.\r\n78: \r\n79: ### 4. Sources of truth\r\n80: \r\n81: List, in priority order:\r\n82: \r\n83: - current user instructions;\r\n84: - governing specifications and approved `Development_Docs` material;\r\n85: - maintained architecture, security, testing, and operational documentation;\r\n86: - canonical code and data directories;\r\n87: - relevant tests and runtime evidence;\r\n88: - external dependencies or authoritative external documentation; and\r\n89: - the rule to apply when sources disagree.\r\n90: \r\n91: State which facts were verified in the current task and which were inherited or require confirmation.\r\n92: \r\n93: ### 5. Repository reality check\r\n94: \r\n95: Before delegation or modification, capture:\r\n96: \r\n97: ```text\r\n98: Git repository root:\r\n99: Canonical remote/repository:\r\n100: Active application:\r\n--- PLANS 101-200 ---\r\n101: Active branch and HEAD:\r\n102: Upstream and ahead/behind/diverged state:\r\n103: Complete working-tree classification:\r\n104: Canonical startup command:\r\n105: Required application port:\r\n106: Relevant focused and full validation commands:\r\n107: Generated, archived, obsolete, or excluded trees:\r\n108: Existing uncommitted work and owner/provenance if known:\r\n109: Safe synchronization action taken or deferred:\r\n110: ```\r\n111: \r\n112: For this repository, verify rather than assume the current baseline. The expected canonical shape is one root Next.js application on `main`, normal startup through `npm run dev:full`, and normal development port `3000`. If reality differs, stop treating the expectation as fact and update the plan.\r\n113: \r\n114: Do not inspect secrets, overwrite local databases, or treat generated validation copies as source. Classify unrelated and pre-existing work before editing.\r\n115: \r\n116: ### 6. Assumptions and unknowns\r\n117: \r\n118: Use this table:\r\n119: \r\n120: | ID | Assumption or unknown | Impact if wrong | Verification method | Responsible lane | Can work proceed first? | Status/evidence |\r\n121: | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\r\n122: | A1 |  |  |  |  | Yes/No |  |\r\n123: \r\n124: Do not silently convert an unknown into a requirement or conclusion.\r\n125: \r\n126: ### 7. Parallelization assessment\r\n127: \r\n128: Answer explicitly:\r\n129: \r\n130: - Which work is meaningfully independent?\r\n131: - Which work depends on another result?\r\n132: - Which lanes can begin immediately?\r\n133: - Which steps must be serialized, and for what concrete reason?\r\n134: - Which resources are shared or exclusive?\r\n135: - Which files or interfaces are conflict-prone?\r\n136: - Which work should be read-only?\r\n137: - Which write lanes need isolated worktrees?\r\n138: - What is the maximum safe and useful concurrency under current capacity?\r\n139: - Why does the chosen decomposition improve speed, coverage, reliability, or context management without redundant work?\r\n140: - What conditions would cause a lane to be split, collapsed, cancelled, or serialized?\r\n141: \r\n142: For a substantial task that remains primarily sequential, document the dependency, safety, exclusive-resource, or overlapping-file reason. Ease of coordination is not enough.\r\n143: \r\n144: ### 8. Dependency graph\r\n145: \r\n146: Represent lane identifiers, prerequisites, fan-out, fan-in, the critical path, and failure independence. A compact text or Mermaid graph is acceptable. For example:\r\n147: \r\n148: ```text\r\n149: P0 repository preflight\r\n150: |-- R1 read-only subsystem inventory --\\\r\n151: |-- R2 read-only test/risk inventory ----+-- I1 coordinator reconciliation\r\n152: `-- R3 read-only requirements review ---/\r\n153:                                          |-- W1 isolated implementation lane --\\\r\n154:                                          `-- W2 isolated implementation lane --+-- I2 shared-file integration\r\n155:                                                                                  `-- V1 integrated validation\r\n156:                                                                                      `-- F1 finalization\r\n157: ```\r\n158: \r\n159: Annotate:\r\n160: \r\n161: - prerequisites and blocking relationships;\r\n162: - ready work and fan-out points;\r\n163: - integration/fan-in points;\r\n164: - the critical path;\r\n165: - serialized shared-resource operations; and\r\n166: - lanes that can continue if another lane fails.\r\n167: \r\n168: Do not force parallel work into a fake sequential checklist.\r\n169: \r\n170: ### 9. Workstream matrix\r\n171: \r\n172: | Lane ID | Lane name | Objective | Agent role | Read/write | Owned scope | Owned paths | Explicit non-scope | Dependencies | Shared resources | Deliverables | Required evidence | Focused validation | Status | Blockers |\r\n173: | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\r\n174: | R1 |  |  |  | Read-only |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  | Ready |  |\r\n175: \r\n176: Every lane must have a non-overlapping evidence or edit boundary. If two write lanes need the same file, change the ownership or serialize the handoff before either edits.\r\n177: \r\n178: ### 10. Subagent briefing template\r\n179: \r\n180: Use a bounded briefing such as:\r\n181: \r\n182: ```text\r\n183: Objective:\r\n184: Background and governing requirements:\r\n185: Scope:\r\n186: Explicit non-scope:\r\n187: Authorization: read-only | write\r\n188: Owned concerns/paths:\r\n189: Resources and paths you must not modify:\r\n190: Dependencies and prerequisite evidence:\r\n191: Constraints and invariants:\r\n192: Required repository-wide searches or checks:\r\n193: Required deliverable:\r\n194: Required evidence (paths, symbols, commands, reproduction, confidence):\r\n195: Focused validation:\r\n196: Return format (compact summary, changes, evidence, tests, blockers):\r\n197: Completion criteria:\r\n198: Stop conditions:\r\n199: Blocker/uncertainty/ownership-conflict reporting:\r\n200: ```\r\n--- PLANS 201-300 ---\r\n201: \r\n202: Briefings must carry the requirements the worker needs; do not rely on the worker to reconstruct user intent. Require distilled results, not full raw logs. A worker must stop and report if its scope becomes ambiguous, overlaps another writer, requires a prohibited shared resource, or would broaden the task.\r\n203: \r\n204: ### 11. Shared-resource and locking plan\r\n205: \r\n206: | Resource | Authoritative owner | Other permitted consumers | Exclusive operations | Conflict-prevention method | Release/handoff condition |\r\n207: | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\r\n208: | Port `3000` and development server |  | Existing logs/HTTP evidence only | Start/stop/restart | Inspect listener and recorded launcher state; one owner | Server stopped or ownership acknowledged |\r\n209: | Browser session |  | Read-only observers if safe | Stateful automation | Separate contexts or one controller | State/evidence saved |\r\n210: | Database and seed state |  | Read-only queries if explicitly safe | Mutation/reset/seed/migration | One database owner; back up when required | State and evidence recorded |\r\n211: | Prisma schemas and migrations |  | Read-only review | Schema edit, migration creation/application | One authoritative writer; preserve order | Schema/migration validated and handed off |\r\n212: | `package.json` / `package-lock.json` |  | Read-only review | Dependency install/regeneration | One package owner | Lockfile diff reviewed |\r\n213: | Global styles / layout / providers |  | Read-only review | Shared interface edits | One integration owner | Consumers reconciled |\r\n214: | Route definitions |  | Read-only inventory | Conflicting route/layout edits | Path owner plus integration review | Route map validated |\r\n215: | Generated assets |  | Consumers of existing output | Regeneration | One generator, deterministic inputs | Output and provenance reviewed |\r\n216: | Full E2E/build/validation |  | Consumers of reports | Expensive full run | One validation owner | Results saved and reviewed |\r\n217: | Deployment environment |  | Read-only status consumers | Deploy/rollback/config mutation | Explicit authority and one operator | Health and rollback state confirmed |\r\n218: | Chat/document synchronization | Coordinator | Workers provide summaries only | Dry-run/live/validate/commit/push | Run once after lanes finish | Structured report and remote state verified |\r\n219: \r\n220: Add task-specific resources. In this repository, normal development uses `npm run dev:full` on `127.0.0.1:3000`. The Playwright default test port (`3100`) and validation production proof port (`3200`) are isolated validation resources, not extra canonical app instances.\r\n221: \r\n222: ### 12. File ownership plan\r\n223: \r\n224: Record:\r\n225: \r\n226: - one writer per file at a time;\r\n227: - path/component/package boundaries for every write lane;\r\n228: - the integration owner for shared files;\r\n229: - read-only reviewers assigned where edit overlap would otherwise occur;\r\n230: - worktree locations or isolation method for independent implementation lanes;\r\n231: - pre-existing files that no lane may stage, overwrite, or normalize;\r\n232: - ownership handoff order and evidence; and\r\n233: - the rule for newly discovered shared files.\r\n234: \r\n235: Recommended table:\r\n236: \r\n237: | Path or pattern | Writer/owner | Read-only reviewers | Ownership window | Dependencies | Handoff condition |\r\n238: | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\r\n239: |  |  |  |  |  |  |\r\n240: \r\n241: Package manifests and lockfiles, Prisma schemas/migrations/seed, root layouts and providers, global styles/tokens, route entrypoints, central registries, runtime configuration, and generated files are presumed conflict-prone until inspected. Exactly one integration lane owns each such resource.\r\n242: \r\n243: ### 13. Runtime strategy\r\n244: \r\n245: Define:\r\n246: \r\n247: - who may start and stop the application;\r\n248: - the exact verified command;\r\n249: - how canonical port `3000` ownership is checked and maintained;\r\n250: - whether an already-running instance can be reused;\r\n251: - how other lanes request runtime evidence without starting duplicates;\r\n252: - how browser testing is divided and state isolated;\r\n253: - where server logs and screenshots are stored or referenced;\r\n254: - how stale or unknown processes are detected and handled safely; and\r\n255: - when runtime validation occurs relative to integration.\r\n256: \r\n257: Repository defaults:\r\n258: \r\n259: - use `npm run dev:full` and `npm run dev:stop` for the normal development lifecycle;\r\n260: - never kill an unknown listener merely to acquire port `3000`;\r\n261: - do not let workers run competing servers;\r\n262: - use Playwright's configured isolated server for E2E ownership, not the shared development server, unless the plan explicitly proves a safe alternative;\r\n263: - treat database-mutating browser work as exclusive unless separate databases are configured; and\r\n264: - centralize final browser/runtime review after integration.\r\n265: \r\n266: ### 14. Testing strategy\r\n267: \r\n268: Plan all applicable layers:\r\n269: \r\n270: | Layer | Owner | Focused or integrated | Parallelizable shards | Serialized resource | Required evidence | Pass/fail treatment |\r\n271: | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\r\n272: | Format/lint/type |  |  |  |  |  |  |\r\n273: | Unit/component |  |  |  |  |  |  |\r\n274: | Asset/contracts |  |  |  |  |  |  |\r\n275: | Integration/API |  |  |  | Database/runtime if used |  |  |\r\n276: | E2E/accessibility |  |  |  | Server/browser/database |  |  |\r\n277: | Production build |  | Integrated | No duplicate full builds | Build cache/output |  |  |\r\n278: | Performance |  |  |  | Target runtime |  |  |\r\n279: | Regression/acceptance |  | Integrated |  | Full gate |  |  |\r\n280: \r\n281: Use only verified commands. Available focused commands include `npm run format:check`, `npm run lint`, `npm run typecheck`, `npm test`, `npm run assets:validate`, `npm run test:e2e`, and `npm run build`. `npm run validate` is the full cross-cutting gate. No separate `test:integration` script is currently verified; describe the actual focused Vitest/Playwright coverage instead of inventing one.\r\n282: \r\n283: Implementation lanes run the smallest relevant checks. Independent unit shards may run concurrently. One validation owner deduplicates full lint/type/build/browser/server work and runs the required integrated gate on the combined state. Record commands, exit status, relevant counts, artifact paths, and failures. Classify failures as pre-existing, task regression, environmental, or unresolved with evidence; never erase failure evidence to make a gate appear clean.\r\n284: \r\n285: ### 15. Integration strategy\r\n286: \r\n287: Define:\r\n288: \r\n289: - integration owner;\r\n290: - lane handoff and merge order based on dependencies;\r\n291: - shared-file update owner and timing;\r\n292: - conflict-resolution process;\r\n293: - how duplicated or competing changes are detected;\r\n294: - how cross-lane assumptions and interfaces are checked;\r\n295: - how the complete integrated diff and working tree are reviewed; and\r\n296: - focused and cross-cutting checks to run after integration.\r\n297: \r\n298: The integration owner preserves both sides of unexpected conflicts, consults the governing source, and resolves only when intent is clear. Workers do not casually merge, stage, or overwrite one another's work. Final Git reconciliation, if authorized, remains serialized.\r\n299: \r\n300: ### 16. Risk register\r\n--- PLANS 301-400 ---\r\n301: \r\n302: | Risk ID | Description | Probability | Impact | Detection method | Mitigation | Owner | Recovery action | Status |\r\n303: | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\r\n304: | K1 |  | Low/Med/High | Low/Med/High |  |  |  |  | Open |\r\n305: \r\n306: Include ownership collision, stale repository assumptions, accidental scope expansion, runtime/port/database contention, migration ordering, generated-file churn, pre-existing failures, security/privacy exposure, incomplete integration, and validation gaps where applicable.\r\n307: \r\n308: ### 17. Decision log\r\n309: \r\n310: | Decision ID | Decision | Reason | Alternatives considered | Evidence | Affected lanes | Date/stage |\r\n311: | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\r\n312: | D1 |  |  |  |  |  |  |\r\n313: \r\n314: Record concise rationale and evidence, not private chain-of-thought.\r\n315: \r\n316: ### 18. Discovery log\r\n317: \r\n318: | Discovery ID | Finding | Evidence | Changes scope/dependencies/ownership/validation/risk | Plan update | Date/stage |\r\n319: | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\r\n320: | X1 |  |  |  |  |  |\r\n321: \r\n322: Log discoveries that materially change architecture assumptions, canonical locations, dependency edges, ownership, validation, risk, or completion criteria.\r\n323: \r\n324: ### 19. Progress tracking\r\n325: \r\n326: Use these statuses consistently:\r\n327: \r\n328: - **Not ready** - prerequisites are unresolved.\r\n329: - **Ready** - prerequisites are satisfied and ownership is available.\r\n330: - **Active** - work is currently running.\r\n331: - **Blocked** - a specific blocker prevents progress.\r\n332: - **Awaiting integration** - lane deliverable is complete but not yet reconciled.\r\n333: - **Integrated** - combined into the coordinator's working state.\r\n334: - **Verified** - required focused and integrated checks passed or were accounted for.\r\n335: - **Complete** - acceptance criteria and reporting are finished.\r\n336: - **Cancelled as redundant** - lane is no longer necessary and useful partial evidence is preserved.\r\n337: \r\n338: Plans reflect actual state, not optimistic expectations. At most one owner writes a file or holds an exclusive resource at a time.\r\n339: \r\n340: ### 20. Failure recovery\r\n341: \r\n342: For each material lane or shared resource, define:\r\n343: \r\n344: - how partial results are preserved;\r\n345: - retry limit or retry condition;\r\n346: - when to narrow, split, or reassign a lane;\r\n347: - when an initially parallel lane must be serialized;\r\n348: - which dependent lanes become blocked;\r\n349: - which unaffected lanes continue;\r\n350: - how ownership and runtime locks are released;\r\n351: - recovery or rollback action for mutations; and\r\n352: - when user intervention is genuinely required.\r\n353: \r\n354: Do not restart completed work because another lane failed. A failed lane returns partial evidence and a precise blocker. The coordinator discloses any gap that remains material to completion.\r\n355: \r\n356: ### 21. Final verification\r\n357: \r\n358: Before claiming completion:\r\n359: \r\n360: - re-read the original user request and governing sources;\r\n361: - map every acceptance criterion to a delivered change or verified result;\r\n362: - reconcile every lane output and resolve or disclose contradictions;\r\n363: - confirm no material agent result remains unreviewed;\r\n364: - inspect the complete integrated diff and working tree for accidental scope expansion;\r\n365: - verify shared-file ownership and handoffs are complete;\r\n366: - run required focused, integrated, runtime, regression, accessibility, performance, security, migration, build, or acceptance checks as applicable;\r\n367: - distinguish skipped, unverified, pre-existing, and failed checks;\r\n368: - confirm generated artifacts and unrelated work were not accidentally staged or treated as source; and\r\n369: - run the single coordinator-owned repository finalization gate from `AGENTS.md`.\r\n370: \r\n371: For documentation-only work, use proportionate document validation and do not launch the application or full test suite unless a governing instruction explicitly requires it.\r\n372: \r\n373: ### 22. Final report format\r\n374: \r\n375: The coordinator reports:\r\n376: \r\n377: ```text\r\n378: Outcome: what was completed\r\n379: Parallel execution: lanes used and why the decomposition was safe/useful\r\n380: Affected files/systems: task-authored changes, sync-owned changes, and pre-existing work classified separately\r\n381: Validation: commands/checks, exit status, and material evidence\r\n382: Failures/limitations: unresolved or skipped checks and why\r\n383: Remaining risks: known risks that remain within or outside scope\r\n384: Follow-up: only work genuinely outside the requested scope\r\n385: Chat archive: required synchronization result\r\n386: Development docs: required synchronization result\r\n387: ```\r\n388: \r\n389: The report provides concise rationale, evidence, decisions, and results. It does not expose private chain-of-thought or paste raw agent logs.\r\n390: \r\n391: ## Standard parallel workflow patterns\r\n392: \r\n393: Adapt these patterns to the task rather than copying them mechanically.\r\n394: \r\n395: ### Repository-wide audit\r\n396: \r\n397: 1. Coordinator preserves the audit prompt, taxonomy, severity definitions, evidence rules, and report format.\r\n398: 2. Fan out read-only lanes by subsystem and/or distinct concern.\r\n399: 3. Give each lane a bounded evidence surface and require paths, symbols, reproduction evidence, and confidence.\r\n400: 4. Use a dedicated verification lane or coordinator pass for severe, disputed, or uncertain findings.\r\n--- PLANS 401-481 ---\r\n401: 5. Fan in through centralized deduplication, severity reconciliation, and contradiction resolution.\r\n402: 6. Produce one coherent audit; do not paste lane reports together.\r\n403: \r\n404: ### Multi-feature implementation\r\n405: \r\n406: 1. Establish shared architecture/contracts and identify shared files.\r\n407: 2. Place genuinely independent feature lanes in separate worktrees or assign disjoint paths.\r\n408: 3. Give shared architecture, schemas, configuration, manifests, and lockfiles one owner.\r\n409: 4. Let each feature lane run focused tests.\r\n410: 5. Integrate in dependency order under one owner.\r\n411: 6. Run final full-system verification once on the combined state.\r\n412: \r\n413: ### Bug investigation\r\n414: \r\n415: Fan out read-only lanes for reproduction, code-path tracing, recent-change/regression analysis, and test-gap analysis. Keep hypotheses distinct and evidence-backed. The coordinator reconciles them and selects a supported root cause before authorizing implementation. Parallel fixes for the same suspected defect are normally redundant unless intentionally comparing isolated approaches.\r\n416: \r\n417: ### Refactor\r\n418: \r\n419: Start architecture mapping, consumer inventory, and test-baseline lanes in parallel. Serialize shared-interface changes under one owner, then fan out independent package/component migrations with disjoint ownership. Integrate in dependency order and run compatibility/regression verification after all consumers move.\r\n420: \r\n421: ### Frontend experience work\r\n422: \r\n423: Use bounded component lanes plus independent design-system/visual-consistency, animation/interaction, accessibility, and performance review lanes when separable. Assign one owner to root layout, providers, shared tokens/global styles, and route shells. Assign one runtime/browser owner. Finish with an integrated responsive, keyboard, motion-mode, console, performance, and UX review against the original acceptance criteria.\r\n424: \r\n425: ### Backend or database work\r\n426: \r\n427: Separate API, repository/service, schema-analysis, test, and operational-readiness lanes where their evidence or files do not overlap. Give schemas and the ordered migration chain one authoritative writer and one database/migration operator. Serialize migration generation/application and shared state mutation. Integrate API/service contracts before final database, security, regression, and deployment-readiness validation.\r\n428: \r\n429: ### Documentation task\r\n430: \r\n431: Fan out read-only source discovery and fact-checking by topic. Topic-specific drafting may run in parallel only with disjoint document ownership. Use one editorial integration owner for shared documents, terminology, links, and final formatting. Verify claims against sources and run diff/format/link checks proportionate to the task. Do not create extra helper documents when the requested output is fixed.\r\n432: \r\n433: ## Anti-patterns\r\n434: \r\n435: Do not:\r\n436: \r\n437: - process independent areas sequentially without a concrete reason;\r\n438: - spawn agents with substantially identical scopes;\r\n439: - make every agent scan the entire repository;\r\n440: - launch work before evidence, path, and resource ownership are defined;\r\n441: - allow multiple agents to edit the same file concurrently;\r\n442: - let multiple development servers compete for port `3000`;\r\n443: - make every lane run the entire test suite;\r\n444: - use agent count as a quality metric or create microscopic artificial lanes;\r\n445: - ignore slower-lane results because faster lanes finished first;\r\n446: - combine reports without reconciling duplicates, severity, or contradictions;\r\n447: - let subagents reinterpret or broaden user requirements;\r\n448: - use parallelism to bypass safety, review, authorization, or validation;\r\n449: - restart completed work after one lane fails;\r\n450: - claim completion before integration and verification;\r\n451: - create alternate application copies, port-specific versions, or abandoned variants;\r\n452: - use generated snapshots or obsolete/source-history trees because they are easier to discover;\r\n453: - hide uncertainty, partial failure, skipped validation, or incomplete integration;\r\n454: - let workers independently fetch/rebase/merge/commit/push or run chat/document synchronization without explicit exclusive ownership; or\r\n455: - stage the whole repository by default in a dirty worktree.\r\n456: \r\n457: ## Adaptive plan behavior\r\n458: \r\n459: Update the plan when:\r\n460: \r\n461: - new dependencies or shared resources are discovered;\r\n462: - two lanes overlap or shared-file conflict becomes likely;\r\n463: - a lane becomes unnecessary or duplicates another;\r\n464: - supposedly independent work is found to be dependent;\r\n465: - a blocked lane can be narrowed or split;\r\n466: - additional safe parallelism becomes available;\r\n467: - runtime evidence contradicts static analysis;\r\n468: - the canonical implementation differs from the initial assumption;\r\n469: - validation reveals a pre-existing failure or regression; or\r\n470: - risk, completion criteria, or user authority changes.\r\n471: \r\n472: When adapting:\r\n473: \r\n474: 1. preserve completed work and useful partial evidence;\r\n475: 2. update dependency edges, ownership, locks, status, risk, and validation;\r\n476: 3. notify affected lanes and stop conflicting writers before reassignment;\r\n477: 4. record the material discovery or decision;\r\n478: 5. start newly ready independent work promptly; and\r\n479: 6. avoid unnecessary restarts.\r\n480: \r\n481: The plan serves the task. It may evolve, but it may never override the user's instructions or governing product requirements.\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

The audit inventories **{len(rows)}** current effects, runtime contracts, and missing opportunities. Of the 28 registered GSAP scenes, **{len(production_scenes)}** have a production call path and **{len(showcase_only)}** are development-showcase-only, dead, or redundant. All 28 can build and finish without a required visible target. Therefore a completed timeline, a green builder test, and a clean showcase run do not prove that a user saw the intended object.\r\n675: The eight scenes above run only in the development showcase. The four production Rive objects do not run as Rive at all: Invitation Seal and Finale Mechanism mount fallbacks, while Journal Clasp and Voyage Compass are contract-only. Modern `PlayerSignIn`, `StaffSignIn`, and `InvitationCeremony` do not call the legacy `player-access`/`quartermaster-login` cinematic paths. The matrix's {category_counts['broken']} broken items include these absent production paths plus infrastructure that makes successful completion unsafe.\r\n768: The baseline is missing **{len(proposals)}** planned moments across **{len(screen_counts)}** screen groups. These are implementation specifications, not claims that product state is absent. They prioritize truthful state transitions and accessibility before decoration.\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

\"undo-reverse\": [210, 74, 0.5],\n};\n\nexport class AudioCuePlayer {\n  private context: AudioContext | null = null;\n  private active = new Set<{ oscillator: OscillatorNode; gain: GainNode }>();\n  private volume = 0.4;\n  private muted = false;\n\n  unlock() {\n    try {\n      this.context ??= new AudioContext();\n      if (this.context.state === \"suspended\") {\n        void this.context.resume().catch(() => {\n          // Audio is optional; a blocked device must never hold the journal closed.\n        });\n      }\n    } catch {\n      this.context = null;\n    }\n  }\n\n  setVolume(volume: number) {\n    this.volume = Math.max(0, Math.min(1, volume));\n  }\n\n  setMuted(muted: boolean) {\n    this.muted = muted;\n  }\n\n  play(name: AudioCueName) {\n    if (!this.context || this.muted || this.volume <= 0) return;\n    const [start, end, duration] = frequencies[name];\n    const oscillator = this.context.createOscillator();\n    const gain = this.context.createGain();\n    const now = this.context.currentTime;\n    oscillator.type = name.includes(\"chime\") || name.includes(\"compass\") ? \"sine\" : \"triangle\";\n    oscillator.frequency.setValueAtTime(start, now);\n    oscillator.frequency.exponentialRampToValueAtTime(Math.max(1, end), now + duration);\n    gain.gain.setValueAtTime(Math.max(0.001, this.volume * 0.11), now);\n    gain.gain.exponentialRampToValueAtTime(0.001, now + duration);\n    oscillator.connect(gain).connect(this.context.destination);\n    const entry = { oscillator, gain };\n    this.active.add(entry);\n    oscillator.onended = () => this.active.delete(entry);\n    oscillator.start(now);\n    oscillator.stop(now + duration + 0.02);\n  }\n\n  stopAll() {\n    if (!this.context) return;\n    const now = this.context.currentTime;\n    this.active.forEach(({ oscillator, gain }) => {\n      gain.gain.cancelScheduledValues(now);\n      gain.gain.setTargetAtTime(0.001, now, 0.025);\n      try {\n        oscillator.stop(now + 0.12);\n      } catch {\n        // The cue may already have reached its natural end.\n      }\n    });\n  }\n\n  close() {\n    this.stopAll();\n    void this.context?.close();\n    this.context = null;\n ([\\\"attention\\\", \\\"ink-heading\\\", \\\"ink-objective\\\", \\\"ink-riddle\\\", \\\"map\\\", \\\"active\\\"] as const)\\n    : ceremonyStages;\\nconst timing = {\\n  omen: 650,\\n  attention: 550,\\n  seal: 900,\\n  parchment: 1600,\\n  \\\"ink-heading\\\": 350,\\n  \\\"ink-story\\\": 1400,\\n  \\\"ink-objective\\\": 450,\\n  \\\"ink-riddle\\\": 900,\\n  map: 700,\\n  active: 250,\\n} as const;\\nconst wait = (ms: number, signal: AbortSignal) =>\\n  new Promise<void>((resolve) => {\\n    const id = window.setTimeout(resolve, ms);\\n    signal.addEventListener(\\n      \\\"abort\\\",\\n      () => {\\n        clearTimeout(id);\\n        resolve();\\n      },\\n      { once: true },\\n    );\\n  });\\n\\nexport function useCeremony({\\n  reducedMotion,\\n  gentleMotion = false,\\n  onComplete,\\n}: {\\n  reducedMotion: boolean;\\n  gentleMotion?: boolean;\\n  onComplete: (event: ClientProgressEvent) => void;\\n}) {\\n  const [stage, setStage] = useState<CeremonyStage>(\\\"idle\\\");\\n  const [current, setCurrent] = useState<ClientProgressEvent | null>(null);\\n  const queue = useRef<ClientProgressEvent[]>([]);\\n  const seen = useRef(new Set<string>());\\n  const running = useRef(false);\\n  const controller = useRef<AbortController | null>(null);\\n  const process = useCallback(async () => {\\n    if (running.current || !queue.current.length) return;\\n    running.current = true;\\n    while (queue.current.length) {\\n      const event = queue.current.shift()!;\\n      setCurrent(event);\\n      const abort = new AbortController();\\n      controller.current = abort;\\n      const stages = getCeremonyStages(reducedMotion);\\n      for (const next of stages) {\\n        if (abort.signal.aborted) break;\\n        setStage(next);\\n        await wait(\\n          reducedMotion ? [\\\"MARK_SOLVED\\\", \\\"Mark Chapter Solved\\\", \\\"Record that the current chapter has been solved.\\\"],\\n  [\\\"AWARD_ARTIFACT\\\", \\\"Award Test Artifact\\\", \\\"Place the Broken Compass Segment\\\", \\\"Draw the next safe development route between revealed locations.\\\"],\\n  [\\n    \\\"REVEAL_ARTIFACT_SILHOUETTE\\\",\\n    \\\"Reveal Artifact Silhouette\\\",\\n    \\\"Expose only [\\\"UPDATE_FINALE_REQUIREMENT\\\", \\\"Update Finale Requirement\\\", \\\"Advance one generic symbolic requirement.\\\"],\\n  [\\n    \\\"UNDO_LAST\\\",\\n    \\\"Undo Last Progression Action\\\",\\n Add stalled-load timeout, fallback metric, mode-continuity and hidden-tab tests.\r\n\r\n### CSS\r\n\r\nKeep texture, paper bow, ink/material state, hover/focus and restrained ambience in CSS. Move `drawer-in`, `runtime-arrive`, transformation ordering and other application/narrative presence into Motion or a bounded GSAP scene. Pause ambient loops when hidden. Ensure one root motion attribute disables transform/continuous motion and does not rely on media rules competing with JavaScript.\r\n\r\n### Web Animations API\r\n\r\nValidate the expected actor exists and is visible; distinguish `no animation expected` from `animation missing`; filter finite relevant animations; race completion with abort and bounded timeout; cancel owned animations on teardown; apply/log a deterministic fail-open semantic pose. Never wait on an unrelated infinite animation.\r\n\r\n### Audio\r\n\r\nFire audio from director semantic labels only after required-target validation, not before `play()`. Map cue, offset, mute/reduced behavior and failure fallback in the scene contract. Current progression has incomplete event coverage; audio must never be the only evidence that a ceremony ran.\r\n\r\n## 17. Massive missing-animation list\r\n\r\nThe baseline is missing **{len(proposals)}** planned moments across **{len(screen_counts)}** screen groups. Player event scenes are section-dependent, and measured examples include zero-height landing stars and duplicated arrival/peripheral targets.\"],\n+        [5, \"Which depend on the Player's current section?\", \"chapter-release and every section event scene: map-reveal, route-draw, artifact-award/connection, quest-discovery/complete, log-entry and finale-tease/requirement. Global event props soften but do not eliminate duplicate/required-content defects.\"],\n+        [6, \"Which run once?\", \"First-arrival/introduction are tab-session gated; unseen progression events auto-present once before viewed acknowledgment; many proposed authoritative transitions are intentionally once per event/mutation.\"],\n+        [7, \"Which one-shot policies are correct?\", \"First-arrival ambience, authoritative acceptance/launch/publish receipts and an event's automatic first presentation are correct when final state remains readable.\"],\n+        [8, \"Which one-shot policies should change?\", \"Historically meaningful progression must gain persisted presentation receipts and explicit replay; transient component/session state cannot be the only replay source.\"],\n+        [9, \"Which cannot replay after refresh?\", \"Chapter release and other Player progression scenes: `lastRelease` is component memory and no persisted replay payload/controller exists.\"],\n+        [10, \"Which scenes have no real trigger?\", \"The same eight no-production-caller scenes; their only trigger is the development harness.\"],\n+        [11, \"Which scenes should be retired?\", \"manual-page-flip and programmatic-page-flip; journal-open should merge into or be retired in favor of the opening-machine contract; unconnected duplicates should be retired unless a product trigger is approved.\"],\n+        [12, \"Which scenes should be connected?\", \"chapter-heading/prose-ink/marker-stamp/ship-course only after a real semantic trigger, dedicated host and ownership contract exist; do not connect them merely to reduce dead-code count.\"],\n+        [13, \"Which behaviors use the wrong library?\", \"GSAP page curl, CSS-authored drawers/route narrative presence, mount-autoplay ink bloom and same-node Motion/GSAP combinations. Section 11 gives the owner decisions.\"],\n+        [14, \"Which nodes have conflicting owners?\", \"VoyageChart marker, ShipsLog entry, TreasureAltar/ArtifactInspection shared element, companion peripheral wrappers, Quartermaster root/overlay and PageFlip source/clone selectors.\"],\n+        [15, \"Which Rive objects are static fallbacks?\", \"Mounted Invitation Seal and Finale Mechanism render static fallbacks. Clone/source selector integrity also needs enforcement.\"],\n+        [18, \"Which CSS phase waits can hang or skip?\", \"Any wait in waitForJournalPhase can skip when actor/animation is missing and hang on an infinite animation; abort cannot break Promise.allSettled without a race/timeout.\"],\n+        [19, \"Which modern platform animations are missing?\", \"Auth, Invitation Ceremony, Player/Captain libraries, New Voyage, waiting room, loading/error/success, shell/route/theme and Studio lifecycle motion; 139 exact additions are specified.\"],\n+        [20, \"What makes the experience unified and premium?\", \"One truth-bearing scene contract, persistent progression host, wrapper ownership, persisted replay, semantic labels/audio, authored Rive heroes, coherent Motion platform transitions and tested reduced/fallback states.\"],\n+        [21, \"What sequence should follow?\", \"Roadmap Phases 1 through 6; Phase 1 animation truth/broken-trigger repair is next and blocks decorative expansion.\"],\n+        [22, \"Which work can be parallel?\", \"After contracts stabilize: independent scene migrations, platform surfaces, asset art, and focused tests on disjoint files. Only exact rows created by the audit run were transactionally removed after a backup and safety assertions. No further mutation run was attempted without a provably isolated database path.\n+\n+### Relevant changes newer than the audit baseline\n+\n+The following main-checkout work existed concurrently and may improve some surfaces after `{BASELINE}`. It is recorded for handoff only, not audited, merged into findings, or treated as validated.\n+\n+{out_of_baseline_table}\n@@\n The layering choices are sound in principle: GSAP for narrative sequencing, Motion for UI presence/layout, StPageFlip for physical curl, Rive for stateful authored objects, Lottie for decorative loops/one-shots, CSS for materials/ambient states, WAAPI for observing CSS completion, and Web Audio for semantic cues. The defect is coordination: target identity, ownership, reduced motion, interruption, final state, replay, and event acknowledgment are not governed by one enforceable contract.\n+\n+### Modern platform versus legacy cinematic paths\n+\n+{modern_legacy_table}\n@@\n ### Motion\n \n Motion is appropriate for presence, layout, shared layout, buttons, cards, tabs, filters and navigation. The baseline uses it in Player and Quartermaster but sparsely in the modern platform. Same-node risks are enumerated in Section 10 and matrix `AM-*`. Split Motion layout/presence to outer wrappers and GSAP/Rive/Lottie visuals to inner nodes; never let both write transform/opacity on the same element.\n+\n+{motion_inventory_table}\n@@\n ### Lottie\n \n All three JSON files validate. Waves and fog loop/autoplay as ambient decoration and pause for element/document visibility. Ink bloom is non-looping autoplay. Because `mode` is a `useEffect` dependency, each mode change destroys/reloads/restarts the player. Remove mode from asset lifecycle where possible; update speed/segment in place. Make ink bloom `autoplay=false` and play it from the `ink` semantic label. Add stalled-load timeout, fallback metric, mode-continuity and hidden-tab tests.\n+\n+{lottie_mount_table}\n@@\n ### Audio\n \n Fire audio from director semantic labels only after required-target validation, not before `play()`. Map cue, offset, mute/reduced behavior and failure fallback in the scene contract. Current progression has incomplete event coverage; audio must never be the only evidence that a ceremony ran.\n+\n+{audio_cue_table}\n@@\n 6. Resolve motion once at the provider and distribute it to every engine. Add project-authored Rive assets behind validated contracts and retain explicit static fallbacks.\n+\n+### Answers to the 23 required final recommendations\n+\n+{final_recommendations_table}\n@@\n-- Full command/output evidence is in `{TRANSCRIPT}` and its source raw transcript; generated runtime artifacts remain ignored under `artifacts/validation/animation-audit/`.\n+- Rendered command/output evidence: `{TRANSCRIPT}` (SHA-256 `7D7E7510A96790F36A1E35256B38F03F41EB62A972F24BA785220CB519C8965B`). Use explicit fallback outcomes; do not weaken required targets to make tests green.\r\n141: - Timeout values that are too short can turn slow but valid accessibility modes into failures; values must derive from scene budgets and remain abortable.\r\n201: GSAP, Motion, StPageFlip, React refs/context, CSS wrapper styling, Rive/Lottie container contracts, and the ownership/visibility utilities.\r\n216: - Unit: claim/release/reject behavior across GSAP, Motion wrapper, CSS transition, Rive container, Lottie container, and PageFlip content.\r\n247: 2. Rebuild `chapter-release` on that host with required seal, parchment, heading/ink, route/quill, completion, and readable fallback states. Keep the current section in place; after an off-Journal ceremony, offer an explicit Return to Journal action rather than forced navigation.\r\n259: - Rive remains on documented static fallbacks until Phase 5; Phase 3 scenes must work with either fallback or future state-machine object.\r\n284: GSAP for narrative scenes, Motion for host/section presence and shared-layout UI, StPageFlip for curl and page state, CSS/WAAPI for physical material phases, Lottie for contained ink/fog effects under semantic control, Rive fallback/state interface, and Web Audio for labeled cues.\r\n292: - Every live event type has a documented and automated every-section outcome: global presentation, optional section enhancement, fallback, acknowledgment policy, and replay/one-shot policy.\r\n303: - E2E: first visit, return visit, skip, back/forward, opening interruption, PageFlip handoff, keyboard turn, orientation change, and fallback.\r\n305: - Runtime counts before/after repeated replay and navigation for GSAP, Rive, Lottie, PageFlip, listeners, and timers.\r\n334: 2. Implement the Invitation Ceremony state flow for resolution/loading, invalid, expired, revoked, PIN, account-required, accept, decline, replacement, and transition to waiting room. Use Motion for form/presence, GSAP for a bounded accepted ceremony, and the Phase 5 Invitation Seal interface with a static fallback until the asset exists.\r\n346: - Phase 5 Rive assets are optional at first because every platform flow must pass with static fallbacks.\r\n367: Motion, bounded GSAP ceremonies, Rive interface/fallback, Lottie ambient effects where contained, CSS materials and restrained pending indicators, Web Audio semantic cues, and dnd-kit for Studio drag/sort.\r\n377: - All modern surfaces have full/gentle/reduced outcomes, failure fallbacks, mobile behavior, and no horizontal overflow at the six required viewports.\r\n410: ## Phase 5 - Rive/Lottie/PageFlip completion\r\n414: 1. Author and register four project-owned Rive state machines while retaining labeled SVG/CSS fallbacks:\r\n416:    - Journal Clasp: `locked`, `awake`, `opening`, and `open` states driven by named opening phases and reset;\r\n417:    - Voyage Compass: `idle`, `bearing`, and `arrived` states driven by normalized bearing/progress and arrival/reset inputs; and\r\n186: - `C` - the full chapter ceremony uses the persistent ceremony host, preserves the current section, and offers an explicit Journal action after completion; it is never allowed to depend on Journal targets.\r\n187: - `+J`, `+C`, `+T`, `+Q`, `+L`, `+F` - in addition to the global presentation, the currently relevant Journal, Chart, Treasure, Quest, Log, or Finale destination settles visibly without becoming a second cinematic owner.\r\n196: | `MAP_LOCATION_REVEALED` -> `map-reveal`              | G       | G+C          | G              | G                 | G          | G              |\r\n197: | `MAP_ROUTE_REVEALED` -> `route-draw`                 | G       | G+C          | G              | G                 | G          | G              |\r\n373: | Long tasks                | each ceremony/transition                                   | No app-attributable long task >100 ms; cumulative long-task time <=200 ms per chapter ceremony and <=100 ms per ordinary UI transition |\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]

That work was treated as out of baseline and left untouched.\r\n33: - Static work covered registry definitions, callers, triggers, selectors, `data-scene-part`, `data-gsap-owned`, `data-motion-owned`, `data-journal-actor`, `data-journal-phase`, state machines, library wrappers, assets, CSS, audio, event mapping, replay and acknowledgment paths.\r\n34: - Dynamic work used an isolated port 3100 only after verifying port ownership; port 3000 was never reused or stopped. Focused tests and live target measurements were retained from the original audit task.\r\n35: - Truth rule: **build/run/complete is not visible success**. Runtime proof requires an expected trigger, correct visible instance, required target count, uniqueness, stable final semantic state, and correct persistence/acknowledgment behavior.\r\n7: The baseline has a substantial animation framework, but it does not yet have animation truth. Chromium and WebKit-mobile first-arrival coverage passed.\r\n506:- **E-06 - live showcase:** all 36 entries exercised in reduced mode, no logged runtime exception; final metrics GSAP 0, Rive 1, Lottie 2, PageFlip 0, assets ready/no failures.\r\n507:- **E-07 - landing:** six required viewports had no horizontal overflow; at 1440x900 stars had zero height and arrival-copy count was two; reduced content remained readable.\r\n508:- **E-08 - journal/PageFlip:** live journal had ten scene-part elements and two visible peripheral targets; PageFlip clone architecture was inspected. No actual event target was observed inside the tested clone snapshot.\r\n509:- **E-09 - authentication acceptance:** baseline wrong-login request returned 401, but `.form-error` did not appear within 10 seconds.\r\n510:- **E-10 - chapter-release acceptance:** authoritative release reached visible seal/parchment/ink stages and exposed replay, but measured 10,081 ms and failed the `<10,000 ms` contract.\r\n511:- **E-11 - isolation boundary:** Next root inference attached the detached server to the main database. Port 3100 was stopped, exact audit rows were removed transactionally after a backup, baseline fields/counts were verified, and no further mutation flow was claimed.\r\n512:- Rendered command/output evidence: `Codex_Chats/chats/019f7612-5f0c-7941-8ee5-cf9390e1a6ea--audit-animation-system-failures.md` (SHA-256 `7D7E7510A96790F36A1E35256B38F03F41EB62A972F24BA785220CB519C8965B`). Generated runtime artifacts remain ignored under `artifacts/validation/animation-audit/`.\r\n513:\r\n514:## 21. Limitations and items requiring further runtime confirmation\r\n515:\r\n516:- The every-event/every-section matrix is a required future test, not a completed runtime claim. Static scoping proves section dependence; it does not identify which duplicate wins in every browser/layout.\r\n46:\r\n47:## Phase 1 - Animation truth and broken-trigger repair\r\n48:\r\n49:This is the exact next implementation phase. It fixes P0 truth and lifecycle failures before any decorative expansion.\r\n50:\r\n51:### Exact scope\r\n52:\r\n53:1. Define a typed scene-target contract for every registered scene:\r\n54:   - required versus optional parts;\r\n55:   - local scene-host and instance identifier;\r\n56:   - minimum visible and unique counts;\r\n57:   - visibility criteria based on connection, rendered box, display, visibility, and effective opacity;\r\n58:   - properties and runtime expected to own each target; and\r\n59:   - explicit reduced/fallback presentation requirements.\r\n60:2. Add a director preflight and typed presentation outcome. `AnimationDirector.play()` must distinguish at least `presented`, `presented-fallback`, `skipped-by-policy`, `aborted`, `missing-required-target`, `interrupted`, and `runtime-failed`. A built or completed GSAP timeline is not automatically `presented`.\r\n61:3. Make progression acknowledgment policy explicit in `PlayerExperience`:\r\n62:   - mandatory ceremonies are not marked viewed when required presentation fails;\r\n63:   - a named, readable accessible fallback may satisfy the policy when the full scene cannot run;\r\n64:   - failed mandatory events remain recoverable instead of being silently cleared;\r\n65:   - snapshot refresh is not treated as proof of presentation; and\r\n66:   - presentation exceptions are logged with event ID, scene, host, mode, and target-integrity outcome without leaking private payload data.\r\n67:4. Add a player-safe persisted replay source for the most recent chapter-release presentation. Prefer the existing sanitized progress event/snapshot data and `ViewedCeremony` model; add schema only if the existing records cannot meet the contract after proof. Replay must reconstruct after refresh and must not call a command endpoint or create progression state.\r\n68:5. Repair `waitForJournalPhase()` lifecycle behavior:\r\n69:   - race each finite wait with abort and a bounded timeout;\r\n70:   - ignore or separately classify infinite ambient animations;\r\n71:   - validate the expected opening actor and finite animation set;\r\n72:   - give missing actors/animations an explicit fallback outcome instead of an unreported pass; and\r\n73:   - settle on skip, unmount, route change, and mode change in a deterministic final semantic state.\r\n74:6. Establish a single reduced-motion decision surface for the director, Motion, CSS state, PageFlip fallback, Rive pose, Lottie frame/segment, and sound policy. This phase establishes correctness and propagation; Phase 6 performs final experience tuning.\r\n75:7. Classify the eight development-only/dead/redundant scene contracts before leaving the phase: `journal-open`, `manual-page-flip`, `programmatic-page-flip`, `chapter-heading`, `prose-ink`, `marker-stamp`, `ship-course`, and `artifact-inspection`. A scene must be connected to a real semantic trigger, retained as an explicit future contract, or deprecated with callers/tests updated. `seal-break` remains a real legacy Quartermaster production call and is not grouped with unreachable scenes.\r\n76:\r\n77:### Dependencies\r\n78:\r\n79:- The audit matrix and full audit provide the 28-scene inventory, target names, call sites, reachability, and severity.\r\n80:- The presentation-outcome type and acknowledgment policy must be agreed before changing `PlayerExperience` or its API tests.\r\n81:- The replay source must use the existing player authorization and event sanitization boundary in `src/domain/visibility.ts`; raw private event payloads must not be exposed.\r\n82:- The phase-wait contract must be stable before Phase 3 changes the physical opening or ceremony handoff.\r\n83:- A mutation-test database path must be explicitly resolved, recorded, and compared with the canonical database path before any integration/E2E mutation test starts.\r\n84:\r\n85:### Files likely affected\r\n86:\r\n87:- `src/animation/core/animation-types.ts`\r\n88:- `src/animation/core/visibility.ts`\r\n89:- `src/animation/director/AnimationDirector.ts`\r\n90:- `src/animation/director/AnimationDirector.test.ts`\r\n91:- `src/animation/director/AnimationProvider.tsx`\r\n92:- `src/animation/director/scene-registry.ts`\r\n93:- `src/animation/scenes/scene-utils.ts`\r\n94:- `src/animation/scenes/arrival.scene.ts`\r\n95:- `src/animation/scenes/access.scene.ts`\r\n96:- `src/animation/scenes/story.scene.ts`\r\n97:- `src/animation/scenes/command.scene.ts`\r\n98:- `src/animation/journal/opening-machine.ts`\r\n99:- `src/animation/journal/opening-machine.test.ts`\r\n100:- `src/components/player/PlayerExperience.tsx`\r\n101:- `src/app/api/player/[campaignSlug]/events/route.ts`\r\n102:- `src/app/api/player/[campaignSlug]/snapshot/route.ts`\r\n103:- `src/app/api/player/[campaignSlug]/viewed/route.ts`\r\n104:- `src/domain/story.ts`\r\n105:- `src/domain/visibility.ts`\r\n106:- `src/lib/snapshot.ts`\r\n107:- `prisma/schema.prisma` and an ordered migration only if an evidence-backed replay design requires them\r\n108:- focused Vitest files and `tests/e2e/animations.spec.ts` / `tests/e2e/acceptance.spec.ts`\r\n109:\r\n110:### Libraries involved\r\n111:\r\n112:GSAP, Motion, CSS, Web Animations API, Web Audio policy, React state, Prisma-backed progress/view records, and the existing animation director utilities. No library consolidation is in scope.\r\n113:\r\n114:### Acceptance criteria\r\n115:\r\n116:- Each of the 28 registered scenes has an explicit required/optional target contract and reachability disposition.\r\n117:- A scene that finds zero required visible targets cannot return `presented`; duplicate required targets outside the declared cardinality also fail preflight.\r\n118:- A mandatory `CHAPTER_RELEASED` presentation is not marked viewed after `missing-required-target`, `runtime-failed`, or an unhandled interruption. An explicit readable fallback may return `presented-fallback` and then acknowledge according to policy.\r\n119:- Replay is available after refresh from sanitized persisted data, works without forcing the Player into Journal, and produces no command, event, snapshot, audit, or additional viewed mutation.\r\n120:- A journal phase with an infinite animation, missing actor, unresolved `finished` promise, abort, or unmount reaches a bounded documented outcome; abort/skip cleanup completes within 100 ms in the unit harness.\r\n121:- Browser reduced motion and product reduced mode both resolve to the same no-travel semantic ordering; `MotionConfig` cannot independently re-enable spatial movement.\r\n122:- Development-only and redundant scene status is explicit in registry/tests; none is presented as production-complete solely because the showcase can run it.\r\n123:- Target-integrity and acknowledgment logs contain identifiers and counts, not private payload content.\r\n124:\r\n125:### Tests\r\n126:\r\n127:- Unit: visibility/cardinality checks for detached, zero-box, hidden, transparent, duplicate, and correctly visible targets.\r\n128:- Unit: director outcomes for success, fallback, missing required targets, duplicate targets, abort, interruption, skip, reverse, and cleanup.\r\n129:- Unit: opening-phase missing actor, no finite animation, infinite animation, never-settling `finished`, timeout, abort, unmount, mode change, and reduced final state.\r\n130:- Component: `PlayerExperience` acknowledgment matrix for every director outcome; the mandatory-event failure remains retryable and the fallback path is readable.\r\n131:- API/unit: persisted replay returns only sanitized player-visible fields and survives refresh without mutating progression.\r\n132:- E2E: chapter release with and without required targets; replay after refresh; replay network log proves no command mutation; reduced-motion semantic order.\r\n133:- Run focused tests in independent shards where they do not share the database. One owner runs mutation-dependent Playwright checks after proving database isolation. One owner runs the final `npm run validate` gate.\r\n134:\r\n135:### Risks\r\n136:\r\n137:- Conflating event receipt, business success, visual presentation, and viewed acknowledgment could create duplicate replays or stall event polling.\r\n138:- A strict target contract introduced before scene hosts exist may initially expose many failures. Use explicit fallback outcomes; do not weaken required targets to make tests green.\r\n139:- Persisting duplicate replay data could leak private payload fields or create a new source of truth. Prefer existing sanitized events and snapshots.\r\n140:- Director type changes affect every scene and consumer and therefore require an ordered migration, not parallel incompatible edits.\r\n141:- Timeout values that are too short can turn slow but valid accessibility modes into failures; values must derive from scene budgets and remain abortable.\r\n142:\r\n143:### Work that can happen in parallel\r\n144:\r\n145:- After the presentation-outcome interface is frozen: target visibility/cardinality implementation, journal WAAPI lifecycle repair, and player-safe replay endpoint analysis may proceed in separate path-owned lanes.\r\n146:- Unit tests for target integrity and opening-machine lifecycle may be written alongside their respective implementations.\r\n147:- A read-only security review of replay payload sanitization may proceed independently.\r\n148:\r\n149:### Work that must not happen in parallel\r\n150:\r\n151:- One owner must edit `animation-types.ts`, `AnimationDirector.ts`, `scene-registry.ts`, and shared scene-builder interfaces in dependency order.\r\n152:- One owner must edit `PlayerExperience.tsx` while acknowledgment and replay integration are applied.\r\n153:- Prisma schema/migration generation and application are serialized under one schema/database owner; do not create a migration unless the existing model is proven insufficient.\r\n154:- Database-mutating E2E, the browser session, ports `3000`/`3100`/`3200`, and the final full validation are exclusive resources.\r\n155:\r\n156:## Phase 2 - Ownership and scene-scoping architecture\r\n157:\r\n158:### Exact scope\r\n159:\r\n160:1. Introduce a dedicated `SceneHost`/scene-instance boundary that provides a local root, unique instance ID, declared target contract, and cleanup lifetime. Director queries must resolve inside that host, not the entire Player or Quartermaster root.\r\n161:2. Turn runtime ownership from warning-only metadata into enforcement. A rejected claim must prevent the incompatible writer from receiving the node. Motion participates in the same ownership model through explicit wrappers, not only `data-*` attributes.\r\n", "type": "input_text"}]
